subjects.name: Brain-Computer Interfaces

subjects.name: Neural Decoding and AI

subjects.name: Neurostimulation

subjects.name: Closed-Loop Systems
subjects.summary: Devices that sense neural activity, decide, and act in real time — feedback that adapts therapy moment to moment.

subjects.name: Neuroprosthetics
subjects.summary: Devices that restore lost motor, sensory, or cognitive function — cochlear implants, motor prostheses, retinal implants.

subjects.name: Clinical Neurotechnology
subjects.summary: Engineering meets patient care — device approval, surgical workflows, monitoring, and translating research into clinic.

subjects.name: Computational Neuroscience

subjects.name: Neuroimaging and Brain Mapping

subjects.name: Neural Implants and Materials

subjects.name: Rehabilitation Engineering

subjects.name: Ethics of Neurotechnology

topics.title: The BCI Loop
topics.explanation: Every brain-computer interface is some version of a four-stage loop: acquire (electrodes sense neural activity), preprocess (filter and clean the signal), decode (a model converts neural features into intent), and act (a cursor moves, a prosthesis grips, speech is synthesized). Feedback then flows back to the user, who learns to drive the system through practice.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Real BCIs in trials today — Neuralink, BrainGate, Synchron, Precision Neuroscience — all follow this loop. Patients with paralysis use them to type, control a cursor, or speak. Loop latency, decoder accuracy, and stability over time are the practical bottlenecks.

vocabulary.definition: Recording neural signals from invasive or non-invasive sensors.
Algorithm that turns neural features into a predicted intent or output.
Visual or sensory result that lets the user adapt their neural commands.
Delay between intent and action — too much breaks usability.
Both the user and the decoder updating together over time.

topics.oneLineMaster: Every BCI is acquire → preprocess → decode → act, with feedback closing the loop.

quizQuestions.prompt: What are the four core stages of a BCI loop?
quizQuestions.explanation: All practical BCIs route signal through these four stages, with feedback returning to the user.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does feedback matter in a BCI?
choices: The user learns to modulate neural activity to drive the system
It records EMG instead of EEG
It is only needed during sleep
quizQuestions.explanation: Skill emerges when the user can see and adjust to the decoder's output.

quizQuestions.prompt: Co-adaptation refers to:
choices: User and decoder learning together over time
Two electrodes recording the same neuron
quizQuestions.explanation: Both the model and the brain change; modern decoders are recalibrated on the fly.

quizQuestions.prompt: What's a real-world consequence of high BCI latency?
choices: The cursor feels disconnected from intent and accuracy drops
quizQuestions.explanation: Above ~100 ms latency, users start to lose the closed-loop feel.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which step turns neural features into intended action?
quizQuestions.explanation: Decoding is the mapping step — regression or classification from features to output.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Mock a BCI loop in Swift: random spike rates → moving average preprocessor → linear decoder → 2D cursor on screen.
topics.relatedFutureLab: CursorBCI Decoder Lab — interactively drive a cursor with simulated neural features.

socraticPrompts.question: If a user's cursor still drifts after long training, where in the loop would you look first?
socraticPrompts.hint: What changes day to day in the brain or the electrode?
socraticPrompts.answer: Likely the decoder — neural representations drift, so recalibration or adaptive decoding is usually the fix.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is closing the feedback loop fast so important?
socraticPrompts.answer: Motor learning depends on tight sensorimotor delays; too much lag prevents the brain from forming a stable internal model.

topics.title: Cursor Control from Motor Cortex
topics.explanation: The classic BCI demo: a paralyzed user moves a cursor on a screen by imagining hand movement. An array implanted in motor cortex picks up spike rates from dozens to hundreds of neurons; a decoder (often a Kalman filter or recurrent neural network) maps those rates to a 2D velocity. With practice, users can click, type, and even draw.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Cursor control was the first BCI capability shown in humans with chronic implants (BrainGate, 2006). It remains the canonical benchmark and the foundation for more complex outputs like robotic arms or speech.

vocabulary.definition: Brain region whose neurons modulate firing with intended movement.
Recursive estimator that smooths predictions using noisy observations.
Decoder that outputs cursor velocity (not position) — generally more stable.
Short session where the user imagines movement to fit the decoder.
Neural pattern distinguishable from movement, used to select targets.

topics.oneLineMaster: Cursor BCIs map motor cortex spike rates to a 2D velocity, then close the loop with the screen.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why decode velocity rather than position for cursor control?
choices: Velocity is more stable and easier to correct mid-movement
Position is illegal under FDA rules
quizQuestions.explanation: Velocity decoders allow the user to continuously steer; position decoders accumulate error.

quizQuestions.prompt: A Kalman filter helps a cursor decoder by:
choices: Combining noisy observations with a motion model
quizQuestions.explanation: It weights new evidence by uncertainty, producing smoother trajectories.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does calibration usually require from the user?
choices: Imagining or attempting cued movements while neural data is recorded
quizQuestions.explanation: Cued imagery anchors the mapping between neural features and intended directions.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which brain area is implanted for cursor BCIs?
quizQuestions.explanation: M1's hand area has strong directional tuning ideal for cursor control.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might a decoder trained yesterday work worse today?
quizQuestions.explanation: Recordings drift due to micromotion and tissue response — adaptive recalibration mitigates this.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Implement a 2D Kalman cursor decoder fed by synthetic cosine-tuned 'neurons'; render the cursor in SwiftUI.
topics.relatedFutureLab: CursorBCI Decoder Lab — VR target-acquisition tasks with a live decoder.

socraticPrompts.question: If the cursor drifts diagonally even when the user imagines 'hold still,' what should you adjust?
socraticPrompts.answer: Likely a decoder bias — recalibrate baseline or re-fit with neutral rest trials.

socraticPrompts.question: Could you decode cursor velocity without ever recording during real movement?
socraticPrompts.hint: What does motor cortex still do in paralyzed users?
socraticPrompts.answer: Yes — motor cortex modulates with attempted or imagined movement, which is enough to train a decoder.

topics.title: FDA Pathways for Neuro Devices
topics.explanation: A neuro device in the US generally moves through one of three FDA pathways: 510(k) clearance (substantial equivalence to an existing device), De Novo (novel low-to-moderate risk), or PMA (premarket approval, required for most implanted Class III devices like DBS and cochlear implants). High-risk implants need clinical trials under an Investigational Device Exemption (IDE).
topics.clinicalRelevance: Knowing the pathway shapes everything: trial design, manufacturing, software updates, and timelines. Modern Class III neuro devices typically need years of trials and PMA — that's why companies plan around it from day one.

vocabulary.definition: Clearance route based on equivalence to a legally marketed predicate device.
Route for novel low/moderate-risk devices without a predicate.
Premarket approval — most rigorous, required for many Class III devices.
Investigational Device Exemption — permission to run a clinical trial.
Quality System Regulation — manufacturing quality standards FDA enforces.

topics.oneLineMaster: 510(k) is equivalence, De Novo is novel-but-lower-risk, PMA is rigorous approval for high-risk implants.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which pathway typically applies to an implanted DBS device?
quizQuestions.explanation: DBS is Class III implanted — PMA required.

quizQuestions.prompt: A 510(k) requires:
choices: Showing substantial equivalence to a predicate device
quizQuestions.explanation: Equivalence to a cleared predicate is the core requirement.

quizQuestions.prompt: An IDE is needed when:
choices: You want to conduct a clinical trial of a significant-risk investigational device
quizQuestions.explanation: Significant-risk human studies require IDE.

quizQuestions.prompt: QSR governs:
quizQuestions.explanation: QSR ensures consistent, controlled manufacturing.

quizQuestions.prompt: De Novo is appropriate when:
choices: The device is novel, low-to-moderate risk, and has no good predicate
A predicate exists with substantial equivalence
quizQuestions.explanation: De Novo creates a classification for novel devices needing less than PMA.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Pick a hypothetical neuro device (e.g., a closed-loop tinnitus mask). Map out which pathway fits and what evidence you'd need.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No future lab — strictly clinical/regulatory; pairs well with all Atlas device subjects.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might a startup choose De Novo over 510(k) even if a marginal predicate exists?
socraticPrompts.answer: De Novo creates a new classification that becomes their own predicate, which can help future variants clear faster.

socraticPrompts.question: What changes about your firmware development under FDA?
socraticPrompts.answer: Software is governed by quality processes — design history files, verification, validation, traceability — every change must be documented.

topics.title: Troubleshooting Implanted Devices
topics.explanation: When an implant misbehaves, the differential is wide: lead fracture or migration, programming drift, battery depletion, electrode impedance changes, infection, or patient-side variables like missed medication. A good troubleshooting sequence is to compare current programming to baseline, check impedances, get imaging if mechanical failure is suspected, and reproduce the symptom in clinic before changing parameters blindly.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Most clinic visits for chronic implants are programming or troubleshooting. Knowing how to think systematically saves time, prevents unsafe parameter changes, and keeps patients out of the OR.

vocabulary.definition: Measurement of resistance at each contact — flags shorts or open circuits.
Physical shift of the lead from its intended target.
End of service — pulse generator near or past its replacement window.
Whether the patient's reported issue can be observed in clinic.
List of plausible causes to investigate.

topics.oneLineMaster: Troubleshoot implants by reproducing the symptom, checking hardware (impedance/imaging), then programming — never the other way around.

quizQuestions.prompt: First step when a DBS patient reports worsening tremor:
choices: Reproduce the symptom and review programming/impedance
quizQuestions.explanation: Diagnose before treating; many issues are programming or hardware.

quizQuestions.prompt: A sudden jump in impedance to >5000 Ω suggests:
quizQuestions.explanation: Open circuits read very high impedance.

quizQuestions.prompt: Lead migration is best confirmed with:
quizQuestions.explanation: Mechanical changes are seen on imaging.

quizQuestions.prompt: Battery EOS means:
quizQuestions.explanation: Time to replace the pulse generator.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is reproducibility important?
choices: It separates real device issues from variable patient factors
quizQuestions.explanation: Chasing intermittent symptoms wastes time and risks unsafe changes.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Make a SwiftUI flowchart UI that walks a user through a DBS troubleshooting tree, branching on impedance and symptom reproducibility.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No future lab — clinical workflow only.

socraticPrompts.question: Patient reports symptoms only at home. What might you suspect first?
socraticPrompts.answer: Environmental interference (e.g., security gates, magnets) or activity-dependent positions, not necessarily hardware failure.

socraticPrompts.question: Why not raise stimulation amplitude before checking impedance?
socraticPrompts.answer: If a contact is short-circuited, raising amplitude can deliver dangerous current densities to a small area.

topics.title: Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop
topics.explanation: Open-loop stimulation runs the same program regardless of brain state. Closed-loop systems continuously sense a biomarker (e.g., beta oscillations, seizure precursors), decide whether the brain is in a state that needs intervention, and adjust stimulation in real time. The promise: more therapy when it's needed, less when it's not — better symptom control with fewer side effects and less battery drain.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Medtronic Percept can sense LFPs and inform programming; NeuroPace RNS detects seizure-like activity and delivers responsive stimulation. Adaptive DBS for Parkinson's that responds to beta is actively in trials.

vocabulary.definition: Measurable signal that reflects a brain state of interest.
Algorithm that flags when the biomarker crosses a threshold.
Time from sensing to acting — must be short enough to matter.
Detector firing when no intervention is needed.
Fraction of time stimulation is on; closed-loop typically lowers it.

topics.oneLineMaster: Closed-loop systems sense, decide, and stimulate — therapy only when the brain says it needs it.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does an open-loop stimulator NOT do?
choices: Adapt to brain state in real time
quizQuestions.explanation: Open-loop runs a fixed program regardless of state.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are false positives costly in closed-loop systems?
choices: They waste battery and stimulate unnecessarily, potentially causing side effects
quizQuestions.explanation: Unnecessary stimulation has both safety and battery costs.

quizQuestions.prompt: A good closed-loop biomarker should:
choices: Track symptoms reliably and be measurable in real time
quizQuestions.explanation: If the biomarker doesn't predict symptom state, closing the loop adds noise.

quizQuestions.prompt: Compared to open-loop, closed-loop typically lowers:
quizQuestions.explanation: Less time stimulating extends battery life and reduces side effects.

quizQuestions.prompt: NeuroPace RNS is famous for being one of the first FDA-approved:
quizQuestions.explanation: RNS detects seizure-like patterns and stimulates back.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Simulate a noisy biomarker. Implement a threshold detector and an adaptive controller; measure false positives vs. missed events.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Closed-Loop Stimulation Lab — interactively tune detector thresholds and see therapy outcomes.

socraticPrompts.question: If you raise the detection threshold, what trade-off do you make?
socraticPrompts.answer: Fewer false positives but more missed events — you have to choose which error is more costly clinically.

socraticPrompts.question: Why can't you just use a perfect biomarker and a hair-trigger detector?
socraticPrompts.answer: Even great biomarkers are noisy in real recordings; aggressive thresholds amplify false positives.

topics.title: Real-Time Seizure Detection
topics.explanation: Detectors for seizures or pre-seizure states usually run on short windows (hundreds of ms to seconds) of intracranial EEG. Features like spectral power in specific bands, line length, or learned embeddings feed into a classifier. Speed matters: a detection that arrives after a seizure has propagated isn't actionable.
topics.clinicalRelevance: NeuroPace RNS and emerging epilepsy devices rely on this. Patient-specific detectors usually outperform generic ones, and most are tuned to minimize missed seizures while accepting some false positives.

vocabulary.definition: Sum of absolute differences in a window — sensitive to high-frequency activity.
Fraction of true events the detector catches.
Fraction of non-events correctly left alone.
Duration of the recent recording used to compute features.
Detector tuned to one patient's recordings.

topics.oneLineMaster: Seizure detection is a low-latency classification problem with asymmetric error costs.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are personalized detectors usually better than generic ones?
choices: Seizure patterns vary widely by patient and electrode location
quizQuestions.explanation: Inter-patient variability is huge; intra-patient features are far more consistent.

quizQuestions.prompt: Latency matters because:
choices: A late detection can't deliver responsive therapy in time
quizQuestions.explanation: If you detect after the seizure spreads, the intervention's window is gone.

quizQuestions.prompt: If sensitivity is 95% and specificity is 50%, the device will:
choices: Catch most seizures but stimulate unnecessarily often
quizQuestions.explanation: Low specificity means many false alarms.

quizQuestions.prompt: Line length features primarily capture:
choices: Amount of high-frequency change in a window
quizQuestions.explanation: Rapid voltage changes inflate the line length sum.

quizQuestions.prompt: Shrinking the window size mostly:
choices: Reduces latency but increases noise in features
quizQuestions.explanation: Shorter windows respond faster but have less data to average over.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Implement line length and band power features on simulated seizure-like data; compare detection latency and false alarm rates.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Closed-Loop Stimulation Lab — race a detector against a propagating seizure visualization.

socraticPrompts.question: Your detector misses seizures from the right hemisphere. What's most likely?
socraticPrompts.answer: The recording electrodes are far from the right-hemisphere onset zone — feature evidence is weak there.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might you intentionally allow some false positives?
socraticPrompts.hint: What's the cost of a missed seizure vs. a small extra stim?
socraticPrompts.answer: Stimulation is reversible and small; a missed seizure is not. Sensitivity is usually prioritized.

topics.title: Firing Rate Models
topics.explanation: Instead of simulating each spike, firing rate models describe a neuron or population by its average rate over time: r(t). A simple rate equation looks like τ dr/dt = -r + f(input), where f is a nonlinearity. These models are cheap, capture much of population behavior, and are great for understanding gain, recurrent circuits, and decision-making dynamics.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Most decoder targets are rate-coded. Many systems neuroscience theories (decision drift-diffusion, balanced networks, working memory attractors) are built on firing rate dynamics.

vocabulary.definition: Spikes per second averaged over a window or trials.
Slope of response to input — how strongly the neuron amplifies.
Function applied to input current (often sigmoidal or threshold-linear).
How fast the rate adapts to a change in input.
Network whose units feed back to themselves directly or indirectly.

topics.oneLineMaster: Firing rate models trade spike detail for tractable dynamics: τ dr/dt = -r + f(input).

quizQuestions.prompt: Firing rate models are best at capturing:
quizQuestions.explanation: They abstract over spike timing to study rate-based computations.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does τ control in τ dr/dt = -r + f(input)?
choices: How fast the rate responds to input changes
quizQuestions.explanation: Larger τ means slower adaptation; smaller τ means snappier responses.

quizQuestions.prompt: A sigmoidal nonlinearity introduces:
choices: Saturation at high inputs and a soft threshold at low inputs
quizQuestions.explanation: Sigmoids cap rate and flatten near zero — biologically realistic.

quizQuestions.prompt: Recurrent rate networks can sustain activity because:
choices: Self-feedback can keep a population active without ongoing external input
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive feedback creates attractor states (e.g., working memory).

quizQuestions.prompt: When would a rate model be a poor choice?
choices: When millisecond-level spike timing matters (e.g., coincidence detection)
quizQuestions.explanation: Fine-grained timing requires spiking or even continuous-time models.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Simulate a 2-population rate model (excitatory and inhibitory) in Swift; plot its response to step input under different gains.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No future lab specific to this — pairs well with SignalScope Lab visualizations.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might you choose a threshold-linear nonlinearity over a sigmoid?
socraticPrompts.answer: It's simpler, has a clean threshold, and matches many cortical neurons over their normal operating range.

socraticPrompts.question: Two rate models with the same τ but different gains — which decides faster on a noisy input?
socraticPrompts.answer: Higher gain accumulates evidence faster, so it decides quicker but is more prone to noise-driven errors.

topics.title: Hodgkin-Huxley in 5 Minutes
topics.explanation: Hodgkin and Huxley's 1952 model describes the action potential with a set of coupled ODEs: a membrane capacitance equation plus three gating variables (m, h, n) governing sodium activation, sodium inactivation, and potassium activation. It accurately reproduces the action potential shape and is the foundation for all detailed neuronal modeling.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Drug effects on ion channels, mutation-driven channelopathies (e.g., some epilepsies), and the impact of stimulation pulses can all be reasoned about using HH-style models.

vocabulary.definition: Variable (0–1) describing the fraction of channels in an open state.
Sodium activation, sodium inactivation, and potassium activation gates.
Inverse of resistance — how easily current flows through a channel.
Ordinary differential equation — used to describe rate of change of a variable.
Disease caused by malfunctioning ion channels.

topics.oneLineMaster: Hodgkin-Huxley = capacitance + three gating variables that turn voltage history into the action potential.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the gating variable 'h' represent?
quizQuestions.explanation: h closes Na channels after activation — drives the falling phase.

quizQuestions.prompt: HH conductances depend on:
choices: Voltage and time via gating variables
quizQuestions.explanation: g(V,t) is the whole point of the model.

quizQuestions.prompt: A channelopathy affecting Na inactivation might cause:
choices: Hyperexcitability and prolonged spikes (e.g., some epilepsies)
quizQuestions.explanation: Without inactivation, neurons can't reset and fire excessively.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are HH models computationally expensive?
choices: You solve coupled ODEs for every neuron at small time steps
They run only in continuous time
quizQuestions.explanation: Stiff dynamics force small steps; networks of HH neurons are heavy.

quizQuestions.prompt: A simplification of HH for large networks is:
quizQuestions.explanation: LIF and exponential I&F preserve the spike-and-reset idea at low cost.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Implement HH in Swift; plot voltage and gating variables during a single spike.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No direct future lab — supports DBS Circuit Lab indirectly.

socraticPrompts.question: If you set h = 1 permanently in HH, what happens?
socraticPrompts.answer: Sodium channels never inactivate, so the membrane stays depolarized and the neuron can't repolarize — no usable spiking.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is potassium current 'slow' compared to sodium?
socraticPrompts.answer: The n gating variable has slower kinetics, so K⁺ conductance ramps up after Na⁺ peaks — that's why repolarization lags depolarization.

topics.title: Linear Decoders
topics.explanation: A linear decoder predicts a continuous output (like cursor velocity) as a weighted sum of neural features: y = Wx + b. Despite being simple, linear decoders are competitive baselines and are robust, interpretable, and fast — which matters when latency and battery are constrained. They're usually fit with ridge regression to handle noisy, collinear features.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Most implanted BCIs in current trials still rely on linear or near-linear decoders for cursor and arm control because they retrain quickly and fail gracefully. Complex deep models only beat them clearly when there is abundant training data.

vocabulary.definition: Numerical summary of neural activity in a time window (e.g., spike rates).
Linear regression with an L2 penalty to reduce overfitting.
Constant offset added to the linear prediction.
Splitting data to estimate how well the decoder generalizes.
How a single neuron's firing changes with a task variable.

topics.oneLineMaster: A linear decoder is y = Wx + b — fast, interpretable, and surprisingly hard to beat.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why use ridge regression instead of plain least squares for neural decoders?
choices: To reduce overfitting from noisy, correlated features
To eliminate the need for calibration
quizQuestions.explanation: The L2 penalty shrinks weights and stabilizes solutions when features are collinear.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does W represent in y = Wx + b?
choices: The mapping from neural features to output
quizQuestions.explanation: W contains the learned weights that combine features into a prediction.

quizQuestions.prompt: Linear decoders often perform comparably to deep models when:
choices: Training data is limited and signals are well-engineered
There are billions of training samples
The task involves long temporal context
quizQuestions.explanation: With clean features and small data, linear methods are hard to beat.

quizQuestions.prompt: Cross-validation tells you:
choices: How well the decoder will generalize to new data
quizQuestions.explanation: Held-out folds estimate generalization, not training accuracy.

quizQuestions.prompt: If decoding accuracy is great on training data but terrible on held-out data, the model is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Overfitting captures noise. Increase regularization or add data.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Generate synthetic cosine-tuned neurons and fit a ridge regression decoder; sweep the ridge penalty and plot generalization.
topics.relatedFutureLab: CursorBCI Decoder Lab — compare a linear and non-linear decoder on the same task.

socraticPrompts.question: When would you prefer a linear decoder over a neural network?
socraticPrompts.hint: Think about training data, interpretability, and clinical constraints.
socraticPrompts.answer: Whenever data is limited, latency is critical, or you need to inspect what the model is doing — which is most clinical BCI settings.

socraticPrompts.question: Cross-validation R² drops from 0.7 to 0.2 after 24 hours. What's the most likely cause?
socraticPrompts.hint: What changes about the recording over time?
socraticPrompts.answer: Neural drift — the feature distribution has shifted. Recalibrate or use an adaptive decoder.

topics.title: Overfitting in Neural Decoders
topics.explanation: Overfitting is when your decoder memorizes the training data instead of learning the underlying pattern. In neuro data this is especially dangerous because trials are scarce, neurons are correlated, and timing artifacts can leak between training and test sets. You'll see beautiful training accuracy and terrible real-time performance — the classic 'works on the lab benchmark, fails on the patient' failure mode.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Many published BCI results have failed to translate because evaluation didn't reflect deployment: trials weren't split by time, neural drift wasn't accounted for, or models were tuned on the test set. Proper held-out testing is a clinical safety issue.

vocabulary.definition: Separating data into a portion to fit the model and a portion to evaluate it.
Penalty added to a loss function to discourage overly complex models.
When information from the test set sneaks into training, inflating apparent performance.
Difference between training and test performance.
Training trick that randomly zeroes activations to discourage co-adapted features.

topics.oneLineMaster: Overfitting is memorization, not learning — split data by time, regularize, and trust held-out scores only.

quizQuestions.prompt: A decoder gets 95% on train, 40% on held-out. What's wrong?
quizQuestions.explanation: Big train/test gap is overfitting — the model isn't capturing generalizable structure.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which split is safest for time-series neural data?
choices: Sequential — train on earlier blocks, test on later
Test on the same trials as train
quizQuestions.explanation: Random shuffles allow temporal leakage; chronological splits mimic deployment.

quizQuestions.prompt: Adding L2 regularization tends to:
choices: Increase training accuracy at all costs
quizQuestions.explanation: L2 penalizes large weights and improves generalization.

quizQuestions.prompt: Data leakage often happens when:
choices: Preprocessing is computed using test data
You record from too few electrodes
quizQuestions.explanation: Normalization or selection done before splitting can leak future info backward.

quizQuestions.prompt: Dropout reduces overfitting by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Dropout forces redundant representations and acts like an ensemble.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Train a decoder twice — once with random shuffling, once with chronological split. Report both numbers and explain the gap.
topics.relatedFutureLab: CursorBCI Decoder Lab — induce overfitting on purpose and watch real-time control degrade.

socraticPrompts.question: Your offline accuracy is 90% but the patient can barely move the cursor. What's the first thing to check?
socraticPrompts.hint: Think about how your offline evaluation was set up.
socraticPrompts.answer: How you split the data — chronological vs random — and whether preprocessing was fit on test data.

socraticPrompts.question: Why does collecting more data help overfitting more than tweaking hyperparameters?
socraticPrompts.answer: More data reduces the variance of the estimate so the model can't memorize idiosyncrasies of a small sample.

topics.title: Brain Data Privacy
topics.explanation: Neural recordings reveal more than intent — they can reflect mood, attention, fatigue, and arguably traits we don't share publicly. Unlike most health data, brain data is generated continuously, can be inferred about even when stored anonymously, and may persist far beyond the original purpose. 'Neurorights' frameworks argue for mental privacy and limits on inference as a distinct category.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Device makers, hospitals, and regulators are still building norms around storage, consent, and downstream use. Chile, Spain, and Colorado have begun encoding neurorights into law. For practitioners, designing minimal-collection systems is a defensive ethical default.

vocabulary.definition: Right to keep one's mental processes inaccessible to outside parties.
Risk that aggregated or 'anonymous' data still allows sensitive inferences.
Collecting only the data strictly necessary for a function.
Proposed human-rights framework for cognitive liberty and identity in the neurotech era.
Patient understanding and agreement before data is collected and used.

topics.oneLineMaster: Brain data is sensitive by default — design for minimum collection, narrow purpose, and explicit consent.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is brain data considered uniquely sensitive?
choices: It may reveal cognitive and emotional states beyond the user's intent
quizQuestions.explanation: Aggregated neural data supports inferences about mood, attention, and traits.

quizQuestions.prompt: Data minimization in neuro devices means:
choices: Collecting only what's needed for the device to function
quizQuestions.explanation: Lower exposure surface for misuse, and easier consent.

quizQuestions.prompt: Neurorights frameworks generally argue for protections including:
quizQuestions.explanation: Mental privacy, cognitive liberty, identity, and fair access are common pillars.

quizQuestions.prompt: Anonymized neural data:
choices: Can still allow sensitive inferences in some cases
Cannot be combined with other datasets
quizQuestions.explanation: Re-identification and inference are real risks.

quizQuestions.prompt: First-line ethical defense in a new neural device is:
choices: Robust informed consent and minimal collection
quizQuestions.explanation: Consent and minimization are universal defaults.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Mock a consent UI: show what data the device records, why, and how long it's kept; let the user opt in/out per category.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No specific lab — every NeuroSim Lab will share these design defaults.

socraticPrompts.question: A wearable EEG app sells 'attention scores' to advertisers. Where does it cross the line?
socraticPrompts.answer: It infers cognitive state and commercializes it without specific consent — a clear violation of mental privacy principles.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is on-device processing an ethical lever, not just a technical one?
socraticPrompts.answer: Raw data that never leaves the device can't be breached, subpoenaed, or repurposed — privacy by architecture beats privacy by policy.

topics.title: Agency, Identity, and Closed-Loop Devices
topics.explanation: When a device senses your brain and acts on it without your moment-to-moment consent, who is acting — you or the device? Closed-loop stimulators in psychiatric trials have raised cases where patients report changes in personality, motivation, or sense of self. These are not abstract questions; they affect consent, liability, and how we evaluate 'success.'
topics.clinicalRelevance: Trial protocols increasingly include qualitative measures of identity, agency, and self-continuity alongside symptom scales. Device designers should think about disclosure, controllability, and gradual exit ramps.

vocabulary.definition: Sense that one's actions are caused by oneself.
Sense that one is the same person over time.
Device that stimulates in response to detected brain states tied to mood or symptoms.
Consent process that revisits as therapy evolves over time.
Framing some neuro therapies as altering the person, not just symptoms.

topics.oneLineMaster: Closed-loop therapies act on you between consent decisions — design for agency, transparency, and reversibility.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might closed-loop stim raise agency concerns?
choices: Device decisions affect brain state without moment-to-moment user input
It only stimulates when the user presses a button
It is identical to open-loop stim
quizQuestions.explanation: Autonomy of decision is partially shared with the algorithm.

quizQuestions.prompt: Adaptive consent means:
choices: Consent process that revisits as therapy or device evolves
quizQuestions.explanation: Ongoing dialog matches the evolving nature of closed-loop therapy.

quizQuestions.prompt: Self-continuity worries are most common in:
choices: Psychiatric DBS trials reporting personality changes
quizQuestions.explanation: Limbic stimulation can affect mood and motivation patterns.

quizQuestions.prompt: A good design response to agency concerns is:
choices: Clear off switches, transparent state reporting, reversible parameters
Hide device behavior from the user
quizQuestions.explanation: Transparency and reversibility preserve agency.

quizQuestions.prompt: Identity-impact measures in trials:
choices: Help capture outcomes beyond symptom scales
quizQuestions.explanation: They complement clinical scales by tracking what symptom scores miss.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Design a SwiftUI 'patient dashboard' that surfaces what a closed-loop device just did and why, with clear off and override controls.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Closed-Loop Stimulation Lab — show the patient's view of the device's decisions in VR.

socraticPrompts.question: A patient says 'I'm not sure if these are my decisions anymore.' What should the team do?
socraticPrompts.answer: Take the concern seriously, document it, consider parameter changes, and offer counselling — identity changes are clinically actionable.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is an off switch ethically important even if it's medically suboptimal?
socraticPrompts.answer: It preserves the patient's authority over the intervention — autonomy is itself part of good outcomes.

topics.title: Recording Electrodes 101
topics.explanation: Recording electrodes come in many flavors: microwires, silicon microelectrode arrays (Utah array), high-density silicon CMOS probes (Neuropixels), flexible polymer threads (Neuralink-style), and surface ECoG grids. Each trades coverage for resolution and invasiveness for longevity. Key engineering parameters are impedance, biocompatibility, mechanical match to tissue, and surface area per contact.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Choice of electrode type drives what signals you can record (spikes vs. LFP), surgical risk, chronic stability, and ultimately who the device can help. Most chronic BCI failures trace back to electrode-tissue interface problems.

vocabulary.definition: Rigid silicon array of ~100 needle electrodes used in many human BCIs.
Electrocorticography — grid on the cortical surface; less invasive than penetrating arrays.
Resistance to current flow at the electrode-tissue interface.
Maximum safe charge per pulse for a given electrode area and material.
Electrode small enough (<50 µm) to isolate single-unit spikes.

topics.oneLineMaster: Pick the electrode by what signal you need, how long you need it, and how much you're willing to risk getting it.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are flexible probes attractive for chronic implantation?
choices: Their mechanical match to tissue reduces scarring
They eliminate the need for surgery
quizQuestions.explanation: Stiffness mismatch is a major driver of foreign-body response.

quizQuestions.prompt: ECoG sits:
choices: On the cortical surface, beneath the dura
quizQuestions.explanation: Subdural grids give broad coverage with moderate signal quality.

quizQuestions.prompt: A very high electrode impedance often means:
choices: A possible open circuit or fibrotic encapsulation
quizQuestions.explanation: Both broken connections and tissue response raise impedance.

quizQuestions.prompt: Charge injection limits are determined by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Materials like PEDOT and IrOx have higher safe charge limits than smooth metals.

quizQuestions.prompt: Utah arrays primarily record:
choices: Deep nuclei from outside the skull
quizQuestions.explanation: Penetrating microelectrodes isolate spikes from many neurons.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Make a SwiftUI calculator that takes electrode area and material, outputs safe charge injection limits.
topics.relatedFutureLab: SignalScope Lab — visualize what each electrode type can and can't see.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might you prefer ECoG over a Utah array for a long-term communication BCI?
socraticPrompts.answer: ECoG is less invasive, more stable over years, and good enough for many communication signals — you trade single-unit access for durability.

socraticPrompts.question: Impedances are climbing six months post-implant. What's happening?
socraticPrompts.answer: Glial scarring and tissue encapsulation are insulating the contacts — a common chronic failure mode.

topics.title: Foreign-Body Response and Chronic Stability
topics.explanation: Anything in the brain longer than weeks faces the foreign-body response: microglia and astrocytes encapsulate the device, neurons recede from its surface, and signal quality drops. Strategies to fight this include flexible probes, anti-inflammatory coatings, smaller cross-sections, and engineered surfaces. None fully solve the problem yet.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Long-term BCI viability depends on managing this response. Devices that work brilliantly on day one and silently on day 365 are the rule, not the exception.

vocabulary.definition: Encapsulation of an implant by astrocytes and microglia.
Brain's resident immune cells; respond fast to injury and implants.
Tissue insulating the implant from neurons.
Surface treatment to reduce protein adsorption and immune attack.
Difference in stiffness between implant and tissue, a driver of chronic injury.

topics.oneLineMaster: The biology fights back — chronic implants live or die at the tissue-device interface.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which cells dominate the chronic foreign-body response?
quizQuestions.explanation: Glial encapsulation is the long-term picture.

quizQuestions.prompt: Modulus mismatch causes problems because:
choices: Stiff implants and soft tissue micromove against each other, sustaining injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Chronic micromotion keeps inflammation going.

quizQuestions.prompt: Neurons receding from an electrode surface causes:
choices: Loss of recorded spikes from those cells
quizQuestions.explanation: Distance attenuates the signal sharply.

quizQuestions.prompt: Anti-fouling coatings work by:
choices: Reducing protein adsorption that triggers immune attack
quizQuestions.explanation: Less fouling means less downstream encapsulation.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which is NOT a strategy to improve chronic stability?
quizQuestions.explanation: Larger cross-sections worsen tissue injury and inflammation.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Plot synthetic spike amplitude vs. days post-implant under different 'response severity' coefficients; explore what coatings do.
topics.relatedFutureLab: SignalScope Lab — simulate how recordings degrade over time.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might a thinner probe last longer in cortex?
socraticPrompts.answer: It induces less injury during insertion and reduces chronic micromotion, both of which lower the foreign-body response.

socraticPrompts.question: Could you fight encapsulation pharmacologically?
socraticPrompts.answer: Yes — local steroid or anti-inflammatory release from a coating can reduce the response, though side effects and finite drug supply are concerns.

topics.title: The Action Potential
topics.explanation: Neurons signal by briefly flipping their membrane voltage. At rest, the inside of a neuron is about -70 mV. When inputs push the voltage above a threshold (~-55 mV), sodium channels snap open, voltage shoots up to roughly +30 mV, then potassium channels open to bring it back down. That ~1 ms spike is the action potential — the fundamental unit of neural communication.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Most recording and stimulation hardware is designed around this signal. Local anesthetics block sodium channels to stop action potentials in pain fibers; antiepileptics dampen runaway spiking; deep brain stimulation electrodes inject current that drives or blocks them.

vocabulary.definition: Baseline voltage across a neuron's membrane (~-70 mV).
Voltage above which an action potential is triggered (~-55 mV).
Membrane voltage becoming less negative or positive.
Brief window after a spike when another spike is hard or impossible.
Property that an action potential either fires fully or not at all.

topics.oneLineMaster: An action potential is an all-or-none, ~1 ms voltage spike driven by sodium then potassium currents.

quizQuestions.prompt: What ion primarily depolarizes a neuron during an action potential?
quizQuestions.explanation: Voltage-gated sodium channels open first, letting Na⁺ rush in and drive depolarization.
quizQuestions.wrongAnswerHint: Potassium drives repolarization, not the initial rise.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the refractory period important?
choices: It sets a maximum firing rate and enforces signal direction
It opens calcium channels at the synapse
quizQuestions.explanation: The refractory period limits how often a neuron can spike and prevents back-propagation along the axon.

quizQuestions.prompt: Action potentials are described as 'all-or-none.' What does that mean?
choices: They either fire fully or not at all
quizQuestions.explanation: Above threshold the spike has a stereotyped shape; subthreshold inputs do not produce one.

quizQuestions.prompt: A drug blocks voltage-gated sodium channels. Expected effect on action potentials?
quizQuestions.explanation: Without Na⁺ influx, the upstroke can't happen — this is how lidocaine blocks pain signals.

quizQuestions.prompt: Resting membrane potential is mainly set by which ion's leak channels?
quizQuestions.explanation: K⁺ leak channels make the resting membrane mostly permeable to potassium, pulling voltage near E_K.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Simulate a leaky integrate-and-fire neuron in Swift Playgrounds: integrate input current, fire a spike when voltage crosses threshold, then reset.
topics.relatedFutureLab: SignalScope Lab — record and visualize simulated action potentials in 3D.

socraticPrompts.question: If sodium can't get in, what happens to the upstroke of an action potential?
socraticPrompts.answer: There is no upstroke — without Na⁺ influx the membrane can't depolarize past threshold.

socraticPrompts.question: Why can't a neuron fire two spikes in immediate succession?
socraticPrompts.hint: Think about which channels need to reset.
socraticPrompts.answer: Sodium channels are inactivated for a few milliseconds (absolute refractory period) and can't reopen until they recover.

topics.title: LFPs vs. Spikes
topics.explanation: Recording electrodes pick up two main signal types. Spikes are the fast (~1 ms) action potentials of nearby individual neurons, captured in the high-frequency band (300 Hz – 6 kHz). Local field potentials (LFPs) are slower (1 – 300 Hz) voltage fluctuations that reflect the synchronized synaptic activity of thousands of cells around the electrode. Spikes tell you who's firing; LFPs tell you what the local network is doing.
topics.clinicalRelevance: BCIs that decode movement intent often rely on spike rates from motor cortex. DBS devices for Parkinson's are increasingly being designed to sense beta-band LFPs (around 13–30 Hz), which spike when patients are symptomatic and can drive adaptive stimulation.

vocabulary.definition: Local field potential — low-frequency aggregate voltage signal near the electrode.
An extracellularly recorded action potential of a nearby neuron.
Algorithms that separate spikes from different neurons by waveform shape.
13–30 Hz oscillation strongly tied to motor control and pathological in Parkinson's.
Filter that keeps a range of frequencies and removes others.

topics.oneLineMaster: Spikes are who; LFPs are what the local crowd is doing.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which frequency band is typically used to extract spikes?
quizQuestions.explanation: Spikes are fast events whose energy sits in the high-frequency band.

quizQuestions.prompt: What does an LFP primarily reflect?
choices: Synchronized synaptic activity of nearby populations
quizQuestions.explanation: LFPs aggregate post-synaptic currents from many cells around the electrode.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is beta-band LFP interesting in Parkinson's DBS?
choices: It correlates with motor symptoms and can guide adaptive stimulation
It is the action potential of dopamine neurons
It is generated only during sleep
It is independent of medication state
quizQuestions.explanation: Elevated beta in the subthalamic nucleus tracks bradykinesia and rigidity.

quizQuestions.prompt: Spike sorting separates spikes by what feature?
quizQuestions.explanation: Different neurons produce distinguishable extracellular waveforms.

quizQuestions.prompt: If your bandpass filter is 1–300 Hz, what signal do you lose?
quizQuestions.explanation: Spikes have most of their energy above 300 Hz and would be filtered out.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Take a sample neural recording (or a generated one), apply a high-pass and low-pass filter, and plot spikes vs. LFP side by side.
topics.relatedFutureLab: SignalScope Lab — flip filter settings live and watch how the same recording becomes spikes or LFPs.

socraticPrompts.question: You're recording from motor cortex and only see slow waves. What's likely wrong?
socraticPrompts.answer: Your bandpass is probably too low — you're filtering spikes out. Raise the high cutoff above 1 kHz.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might LFP be more reliable than spikes over years of implantation?
socraticPrompts.hint: Consider what happens to electrodes over time.
socraticPrompts.answer: Single-unit spikes degrade as electrodes drift or scar; LFPs aggregate over a wider area and stay measurable for longer.

topics.title: fMRI and the BOLD Signal
topics.explanation: Functional MRI doesn't measure neural activity directly — it measures blood oxygenation. Active neurons demand more oxygen, the local vasculature overshoots in delivering oxygenated hemoglobin, and the resulting magnetic susceptibility change is what BOLD (blood-oxygen-level-dependent) imaging picks up. The hemodynamic response is slow (peaks ~5–6 s after activity), which limits temporal resolution.
topics.clinicalRelevance: fMRI is used for pre-surgical mapping of motor and language areas, research into brain networks, and studies of patient populations. Its slow hemodynamics mean it's not for real-time control, but its spatial resolution is excellent.

vocabulary.definition: Blood-oxygen-level-dependent contrast — the basis of standard fMRI.
Slow blood-flow change following neural activity.
3D pixel — the basic spatial unit of an fMRI volume.
General linear model used to detect task-evoked BOLD changes.
fMRI used to localize eloquent cortex before brain surgery.

topics.oneLineMaster: fMRI sees blood, not spikes — high spatial resolution, slow time, indirect proxy of neural activity.

quizQuestions.prompt: BOLD measures:
quizQuestions.explanation: BOLD reflects hemodynamic response to activity, not the spikes themselves.

quizQuestions.prompt: The peak of the hemodynamic response is roughly:
quizQuestions.explanation: This slow response is why fMRI has poor temporal resolution.

quizQuestions.prompt: fMRI's strongest feature compared to EEG is:
quizQuestions.explanation: EEG wins on speed; fMRI wins on millimeter-scale localization.

quizQuestions.prompt: Pre-surgical mapping uses fMRI to:
choices: Localize eloquent cortex relative to the lesion
quizQuestions.explanation: Surgeons use it to plan resections that spare critical function.

quizQuestions.prompt: A GLM in fMRI analysis is used to:
choices: Detect voxels whose timeseries match a task design
quizQuestions.explanation: The model regresses voxel timeseries on the expected response.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Make a SwiftUI animation that overlays a slow hemodynamic curve on top of a fast neural firing trace to show the temporal mismatch.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No direct lab — pairs with Closed-Loop Stimulation Lab when discussing biomarker speed.

socraticPrompts.question: Why can't fMRI be used to drive a fast cursor BCI?
socraticPrompts.answer: The BOLD response is too slow — by the time you see activity, the user's intent is already seconds old.

socraticPrompts.question: How does fMRI compare to invasive recording for spatial precision?
socraticPrompts.answer: fMRI gives millimeter-scale 3D coverage; invasive electrodes give sub-millimeter local detail. They are complementary, not redundant.

topics.title: Lesion Mapping and Function
topics.explanation: Mapping deficits onto lesion locations is one of the oldest tools of neurology. By systematically comparing where lesions are and what functions are lost, we can infer that a region is necessary for a function. Modern lesion-symptom mapping uses voxel-wise statistics over many patients to make these inferences quantitatively.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Used in stroke neurology, neurosurgical planning, and TMS targeting. Lesion network mapping (Fox lab and others) extends single-lesion findings to whole-network signatures of disorders.

vocabulary.definition: Localized tissue damage from stroke, tumor, trauma, etc.
Lesion evidence tells us a region is necessary; imaging tells us it is involved.
Statistical mapping of lesion locations against behavior.
Method that finds the common network connected to lesions causing a symptom.
Tissue whose damage produces serious functional loss.

topics.oneLineMaster: Lesion mapping tells us what a brain region is necessary for, in a way activation imaging alone cannot.

quizQuestions.prompt: Lesion evidence is uniquely strong because it shows:
choices: Necessity of a region for a function
quizQuestions.explanation: Loss of function with loss of region implies necessity.

quizQuestions.prompt: Voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping requires:
choices: Many patients with varied lesion locations and standardized behavior measures
quizQuestions.explanation: Statistical power comes from variation across patients.

quizQuestions.prompt: Lesion network mapping extends single-lesion findings by:
choices: Finding the common network connected to many lesions causing a symptom
quizQuestions.explanation: It links lesion locations to a shared network signature.

quizQuestions.prompt: fMRI activation alone tells you a region is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Many involved regions aren't required — lesion data tightens the inference.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which clinical use relies most directly on lesion-symptom data?
choices: Preoperative planning to avoid eloquent cortex
quizQuestions.explanation: Surgical decisions about safe resection margins lean heavily on this evidence.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Build a SwiftUI map of brain regions; clicking a region 'lesions' it and toggles which functions are listed as impaired.
topics.relatedFutureLab: No direct lab — informs DBS Circuit Lab targeting choices.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might two patients with similar lesions have very different deficits?
socraticPrompts.answer: Pre-existing variability, plasticity-driven reorganization, and exact white-matter involvement vary across individuals.

socraticPrompts.question: Could a deficit be caused by a structurally intact region?
socraticPrompts.answer: Yes — a distant lesion can disconnect or de-tune an intact region, producing apparent dysfunction (diaschisis).

topics.title: Motor Neuroprostheses
topics.explanation: A motor neuroprosthesis restores movement to people with paralysis by reading intent from the nervous system and driving an actuator — a robotic arm, an exoskeleton, or the user's own muscles via functional electrical stimulation. Signals can come from cortex (BCI), peripheral nerves, or residual muscles (EMG). Practical systems combine high-DOF control with shared autonomy so the device handles low-level motor planning.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Used in trials and select clinics for tetraplegia, ALS, and amputation. The hardest engineering problems are robust multi-DOF control, calibration over time, and giving the user some sense of body ownership over the device.

vocabulary.definition: Degrees of freedom — independently controllable axes (e.g., shoulder pitch, yaw).
Functional electrical stimulation of muscles to produce movement.
Robot handles low-level details while the user provides high-level intent.
Sense that the device is part of one's body.
Electromyography — recording electrical activity from muscle.

topics.oneLineMaster: Motor prostheses decode intent and let an actuator do the work — usually with shared autonomy.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does shared autonomy help in high-DOF prostheses?
choices: It reduces cognitive load by automating low-level motor details
It eliminates the need for batteries
It removes the user from the loop
quizQuestions.explanation: Users provide intent; the device handles grasp shaping, balance, etc.

quizQuestions.prompt: FES drives movement by:
choices: Stimulating peripheral nerves or muscles directly
quizQuestions.explanation: Pulses depolarize motor axons or muscle fibers to produce contraction.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which is a major challenge for chronic motor BCIs?
choices: Maintaining decoder accuracy as neural recordings drift
quizQuestions.explanation: Daily drift forces frequent recalibration or adaptive decoding.

quizQuestions.prompt: Embodiment in a prosthesis refers to:
choices: The user feeling the device as part of their body
quizQuestions.explanation: Embodiment improves with sensory feedback and predictable control.

quizQuestions.prompt: EMG control is most useful for users who:
choices: Retain residual muscle activity above the lesion or stump
quizQuestions.explanation: EMG depends on residual contractile activity.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Design a SwiftUI control panel that mixes intent (from a slider) with autonomy (auto-grasp toggle) for a virtual 2-DOF arm.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Prosthetic Grip Lab — VR pick-and-place tasks driven by simulated decoder intent.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is 7-DOF arm control still hard even with great cortical signals?
socraticPrompts.answer: Coordinating 7 axes simultaneously is mentally exhausting — shared autonomy and lower-level constraints make it usable.

socraticPrompts.question: Where would you add sensory feedback first in a motor prosthesis?
socraticPrompts.hint: Think about what users miss most.
socraticPrompts.answer: Grip force and contact onset — small sensory cues make object manipulation dramatically more reliable.

topics.title: Sensory Feedback in Prosthetics
topics.explanation: Without sensation, prosthetic users can't tell how hard they're gripping or when they've made contact — they have to watch the device constantly. Adding feedback can be done with peripheral nerve stimulation (e.g., LIFE/TIME electrodes), targeted reinnervation, or non-invasive vibrotactile sleeves. Even crude feedback meaningfully improves performance and a sense of ownership.
topics.clinicalRelevance: Sensory-enabled prostheses reduce user fatigue and increase real-world adoption. Clinical trials of intraneural feedback have shown improved object identification and texture discrimination.

vocabulary.definition: Electrode inserted into a peripheral nerve to stimulate sensory fibers.
Surgery to reroute residual nerves to new muscle/skin sites for control and sensation.
Non-invasive feedback via skin vibration.
Map of perceived locations on the missing limb.
Prosthesis where output sensors drive sensory feedback in real time.

topics.oneLineMaster: Even simple sensory feedback transforms a prosthesis from a tool you watch to a limb you trust.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does even crude sensory feedback help so much?
choices: It frees the user from constantly watching the device
quizQuestions.explanation: Lower visual attention cost is huge for everyday function.

quizQuestions.prompt: Intraneural electrodes work by:
choices: Stimulating sensory fibers within a peripheral nerve
quizQuestions.explanation: Direct activation of sensory axons produces perceived touch.

quizQuestions.prompt: Targeted reinnervation surgery is often used to:
choices: Reroute nerves to new muscle/skin sites for control and sensation
Avoid the need for any electrodes
quizQuestions.explanation: It creates new EMG sites and a touch surface on the chest or stump.

quizQuestions.prompt: Vibrotactile feedback's main advantage is:
choices: It produces natural perception of texture
quizQuestions.explanation: It's easy to deploy, though the sensation isn't natural.

quizQuestions.prompt: A closed-loop prosthesis closes the loop with:
choices: Real-time sensory feedback driven by output sensors
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensors at the device output feed user feedback continuously.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Prototype a sensory-feedback map in SwiftUI: as virtual fingers press, illuminate a corresponding zone on a body diagram.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Prosthetic Grip Lab — toggle feedback on/off and time pick-and-place tasks.

socraticPrompts.question: If a user reports tingling in the wrong finger, what's the issue?
socraticPrompts.answer: The stimulating contact lands on a different sensory fascicle than expected — remap by choosing different contacts.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might a user prefer vibrotactile feedback over invasive feedback at first?
socraticPrompts.answer: It avoids surgery and lets them trial whether sensation meaningfully helps their use case before going invasive.

topics.title: Deep Brain Stimulation Basics
topics.explanation: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) delivers continuous high-frequency current (typically 130 Hz, 60–90 µs pulse width, a few volts) through electrodes implanted in deep nuclei. For Parkinson's, the subthalamic nucleus (STN) or globus pallidus interna (GPi) is targeted; the stimulation effectively quiets pathological oscillations and dramatically reduces tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia.
topics.clinicalRelevance: DBS is FDA-approved for Parkinson's, essential tremor, dystonia, OCD, and epilepsy. Surgery places leads stereotactically; a pulse generator under the collarbone runs the program. Programming sessions trade off symptom control vs. side effects like dysarthria, mood changes, and tingling.

vocabulary.definition: Subthalamic nucleus — common DBS target for Parkinson's motor symptoms.
Duration of each electrical pulse, usually 60–90 µs.
Voltage or current of stimulation — main lever during programming.
Charge per pulse per electrode area — kept below safety limits to avoid tissue damage.
Clinic visit where contacts, amplitude, and frequency are tuned.

topics.oneLineMaster: DBS quiets pathological oscillations with continuous high-frequency current at a chosen deep target.

quizQuestions.prompt: What's a common DBS target for Parkinson's motor symptoms?
quizQuestions.explanation: STN and GPi are the workhorse targets.

quizQuestions.prompt: Typical DBS frequency is around:
quizQuestions.explanation: High-frequency stimulation (~100–185 Hz) is therapeutic; low-frequency is generally not.

quizQuestions.prompt: Which side effect is common when amplitude is too high?
quizQuestions.explanation: Spreading current activates nearby corticospinal or sensory fibers.

quizQuestions.prompt: Charge density limits matter because:
quizQuestions.explanation: Crossing safe charge density thresholds causes irreversible tissue injury.

quizQuestions.prompt: DBS is FDA-approved for all of these EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.explanation: DBS is for movement and certain psychiatric/epilepsy indications; diabetes is not one.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Build a programming-session simulator: slider for amplitude, pulse width, frequency; mock side-effect zones light up as values exceed thresholds.
topics.relatedFutureLab: DBS Circuit Lab — visualize current spread from a lead in 3D anatomy.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is high-frequency stimulation therapeutic but low-frequency often is not?
socraticPrompts.answer: High-frequency stim disrupts the synchronized beta rhythm; low-frequency stim can actually reinforce it.

socraticPrompts.question: Patient reports tingling on the left hand at 3.0 V. What's the most likely cause?
socraticPrompts.answer: Current spreading laterally into the internal capsule, activating sensory fibers — reduce amplitude or switch contacts.

topics.title: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS)
topics.explanation: TMS uses a coil held over the scalp to generate a brief, strong magnetic field, which induces an electric current in cortical tissue and depolarizes neurons non-invasively. Repetitive TMS (rTMS) at certain frequencies can transiently increase or decrease cortical excitability, which is used to treat depression and map motor function.
topics.clinicalRelevance: rTMS is FDA-cleared for treatment-resistant depression, OCD, and migraine. Single-pulse TMS to motor cortex is also used pre-surgically to map eloquent areas. It is non-invasive, awake, and doesn't require anesthesia.

vocabulary.definition: Shape of the TMS coil (figure-8, H-coil) which determines focality and depth.
Minimum intensity to evoke a small twitch in a target muscle.
Repetitive TMS — trains of pulses that can durably modulate excitability.
TMS protocol that compresses treatment into a few minutes per session.
Cortex whose damage causes severe deficits — speech, motor, vision.

topics.oneLineMaster: TMS uses pulsed magnetic fields to non-invasively induce cortical currents that can map or modulate networks.

quizQuestions.prompt: What physical principle lets TMS reach the brain through the skull?
choices: Magnetic fields pass through bone and induce currents in tissue
quizQuestions.explanation: Magnetic fields penetrate skull, then induce currents per Faraday's law.

quizQuestions.prompt: rTMS is FDA-cleared for:
quizQuestions.explanation: Depression, OCD, and migraine are the major cleared indications.

quizQuestions.prompt: Motor threshold is determined by:
choices: The minimum intensity that evokes a small target-muscle twitch
quizQuestions.explanation: It calibrates dose to each person's cortex.

quizQuestions.prompt: A figure-8 coil gives:
choices: Better focality than a circular coil
Deeper stimulation than any other coil
quizQuestions.explanation: Figure-8 focuses the field at the intersection of the two windings.

quizQuestions.prompt: Theta burst stimulation is attractive clinically because:
choices: Sessions can be very short while remaining effective
quizQuestions.explanation: iTBS compresses depression treatment to ~3 minutes per session.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Code a 2D map of induced currents under a figure-8 coil for a few coil orientations; compare focality.
topics.relatedFutureLab: DBS Circuit Lab — TMS extension: visualize induced E-fields on a brain mesh.

socraticPrompts.question: Why do we always determine motor threshold before treating depression?
socraticPrompts.hint: Brains differ in skull thickness and cortical geometry.
socraticPrompts.answer: It personalizes the stimulation dose so the same percentage of MT gives a comparable cortical effect.

socraticPrompts.question: What limits how deep TMS can stimulate?
socraticPrompts.hint: Think about field strength vs. distance and safety.
socraticPrompts.answer: Magnetic fields decay quickly with distance from the coil; reaching deeper structures requires higher intensity that also activates superficial cortex.

topics.title: Engineering for Stroke Rehabilitation
topics.explanation: After stroke, recovery depends on driving the surviving nervous system to take on lost function — guided by intense, task-specific practice. Engineering helps by providing devices that allow more reps (robotics), motivate practice (games, VR), give feedback the patient and clinician can interpret (sensors), or directly augment plasticity (BCIs, FES, stimulation).
topics.clinicalRelevance: Stroke is a leading cause of long-term disability. Rehabilitation devices that increase practice volume and intensity show real outcome gains; the design challenges are adherence, individualization, and integrating into clinic workflows.

vocabulary.definition: Practicing the actual movement the patient wants to regain.
The nervous system's ability to reorganize after injury or practice.
Exoskeleton or treadmill robot that assists stepping practice.
Visual illusion that helps recover function in a hemiparetic limb.
Brain signal triggers electrical stimulation of muscles to drive movement.

topics.oneLineMaster: Rehab engineering wins by enabling more, better-targeted, well-motivated practice — not by replacing the brain.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is task-specific training emphasized?
choices: Plasticity is shaped by the activity actually practiced
quizQuestions.explanation: The brain reorganizes around what it does, so practice the target task.

quizQuestions.prompt: BCI-FES systems help by:
choices: Linking intent to muscle activation, encouraging plasticity around movement
quizQuestions.explanation: Closing the loop reinforces neural pathways via paired stimulation.

quizQuestions.prompt: A robotic gait trainer's main contribution is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Volume and consistency are the levers.

quizQuestions.prompt: Mirror therapy works by:
choices: Providing visual feedback that the affected limb is moving
quizQuestions.explanation: Visual illusion engages plasticity in motor and somatosensory areas.

quizQuestions.prompt: Adherence is critical because:
choices: Plasticity needs sustained practice over weeks to months
quizQuestions.explanation: Devices only help if patients use them — designing for adherence is core.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Build a SwiftUI game where the user 'practices' a movement (slider/gesture) and earns adaptive difficulty as accuracy climbs.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Prosthetic Grip Lab and CursorBCI Decoder Lab share the rehabilitation feedback patterns.

socraticPrompts.question: Why might a perfect robot that does the movement *for* the patient hurt recovery?
socraticPrompts.answer: Plasticity needs the patient's neural drive — too much passive assistance reduces neural engagement and gain.

socraticPrompts.question: How would you design a rehab device to maximize adherence?
socraticPrompts.answer: Short sessions, clear feedback, meaningful goals, gamification, and integration into daily routines.

topics.title: Functional Electrical Stimulation
topics.explanation: FES uses surface or implanted electrodes to stimulate motor nerves, contracting paralyzed muscles for functional movement — grasping, walking, breathing. Pulses are tuned for selectivity (which fiber type and which muscle) and to delay fatigue. FES can be paired with BCI control or volitional triggers like EMG.
topics.clinicalRelevance: FES is used for foot-drop after stroke, hand grasp after spinal cord injury, diaphragm pacing, and to prevent disuse atrophy. The biggest engineering challenges are fatigue, selectivity, and natural-feeling control.

vocabulary.definition: A motor neuron and the muscle fibers it innervates.
Order in which motor units are activated; FES reverses the natural order.
Ability to stimulate one muscle without spillover into neighbors.
Drop in force output as stimulation continues — a major FES limitation.
Inability to lift the foot during gait; classic FES indication.

topics.oneLineMaster: FES drives paralyzed muscles directly; fatigue and selectivity are its hardest problems.

quizQuestions.prompt: FES reverses natural recruitment order, meaning:
choices: Large fatigable fibers are activated first
quizQuestions.explanation: Electrical thresholds favor large axons, opposite of voluntary recruitment.

quizQuestions.prompt: Foot drop after stroke is often treated with:
choices: FES to the peroneal nerve synchronized with gait
quizQuestions.explanation: Stimulation timed to swing phase lifts the toes.

quizQuestions.prompt: Selectivity matters because:
choices: Spillover activates wrong muscles and disrupts movement
quizQuestions.explanation: Activating antagonists or wrong muscles makes the movement useless.

quizQuestions.prompt: A reason FES is paired with BCI in some systems:
choices: Neural intent triggers the stimulation, closing a sensorimotor loop
It removes the need for electrodes
It eliminates the need for FDA approval
quizQuestions.explanation: Volitional triggers improve usability and may enhance plasticity.

quizQuestions.prompt: Major FES limitation:
quizQuestions.explanation: Recruiting fatigable fibers first burns them out quickly.

topics.miniProjectIdea: Simulate a fatigue model: stimulate at a fixed rate, track force output declining over time, then try interleaved stimulation to delay fatigue.
topics.relatedFutureLab: Prosthetic Grip Lab and Closed-Loop Stimulation Lab can be adapted to demonstrate FES principles.

socraticPrompts.question: How could you delay FES muscle fatigue?
socraticPrompts.answer: Interleave stimulation across motor unit pools so any one population gets rest periods.

socraticPrompts.question: Why is paired BCI-FES potentially more rehabilitative than passive FES?
socraticPrompts.answer: When neural intent precedes muscle activation by a small delay, it strengthens the spared corticospinal pathways through pairing.

learningPaths.title: BCI Builder Path
learningPaths.summary: A guided vertical slice for learning how neural signals become useful BCI control, from raw time-series data through decoder drift, feedback, UX, and brain-data ethics.

steps.title: Neural data as time series
steps.reflectionQuestion: What makes a neural recording different from a single voltage value?
steps.stepObjective: Recognize neural recordings as ordered voltage samples over time.
steps.explanation: BCI systems start with measurements over time. Learning to see samples as a time series makes spikes, noise, drift, and features easier to reason about.
steps.oneLineMaster: A BCI begins by turning neural activity into time-stamped data.

steps.title: Spike detection
steps.reflectionQuestion: How could a threshold mistake turn noise into fake spikes?
steps.stepObjective: Practice scanning a signal for threshold crossings that could represent spikes.
steps.explanation: Many invasive BCIs depend on separating real action potentials from baseline noise. Detection is the first place signal reasoning can succeed or fail.
steps.oneLineMaster: Spike detection asks whether a waveform is neural activity or noise.

steps.title: Firing rate
steps.reflectionQuestion: Why is spikes per second easier for a decoder to use than raw spike timing?
steps.stepObjective: Convert spike counts and time windows into a reusable firing-rate calculation.
steps.explanation: Firing rate compresses many spikes into a rate that can drive models and decoders. It is one of the cleanest bridges from biology to computation.
steps.oneLineMaster: Firing rate turns spike timing into a decoder-friendly number.

steps.title: Signal noise
steps.reflectionQuestion: What evidence would convince you that an event is artifact instead of neural activity?
steps.stepObjective: Separate neural signal from movement, electrical, cardiac, or biological artifact.
steps.explanation: Noise is unwanted variation in the recording: movement artifact, electrical interference, cardiac contamination, electrode instability, or unrelated biological activity. Bad signal-to-noise makes a decoder learn the wrong thing.
steps.oneLineMaster: Signal noise is messy non-intent data that can quietly break a decoder.

steps.title: Filtering and smoothing
steps.reflectionQuestion: How could smoothing help an LFP feature but hurt spike timing?
steps.stepObjective: Understand preprocessing as cleaning signal without deleting task-relevant information.
steps.explanation: Filtering and smoothing are preprocessing steps. They reduce unwanted frequencies or local jumps, but every filter is a trade-off: clean enough to decode, not so aggressive that spikes, timing, or frequency-band evidence disappears.
steps.oneLineMaster: Filtering is useful only when it removes noise while preserving the feature you need.

steps.title: Feature extraction
steps.reflectionQuestion: Which feature would you try first for left vs. right cursor intent?
steps.stepObjective: Turn raw or filtered neural data into compact numbers such as amplitude, power, spike count, and firing rate.
steps.explanation: Features are compact measurements, such as firing rate, band power, spike count, peak amplitude, or frequency content, that a decoder can use. Good features often matter more than a fancy model.
steps.oneLineMaster: Feature extraction converts raw neural data into model-ready evidence.

steps.title: Simple decoder logic
steps.reflectionQuestion: What could make a simple left/right decoder reliable offline but weak in real use?
steps.stepObjective: Map extracted features to intent or action with a rule, threshold, or classifier.
steps.explanation: A decoder maps neural features to intended action. Even simple left/right threshold logic teaches the core idea behind more advanced BCI models, and the linked simulation shows why reliability matters.
steps.oneLineMaster: A decoder is the rule or model that turns neural features into commands.

steps.title: Cursor-control BCI
steps.reflectionQuestion: Why does screen feedback change how the user learns the decoder?
steps.stepObjective: Connect neural decoding to visible cursor movement and user feedback.
steps.explanation: Cursor control ties decoding to visible feedback. The user learns through the loop, so latency, calibration, and output feel all matter.
steps.oneLineMaster: Cursor BCIs work when decoded intent and fast feedback form a usable loop.

steps.title: Calibration and decoder drift
steps.checkpointPrompt: Checkpoint A: What can cause a decoder to drift? How would you detect it? What would you recalibrate first?
steps.reflectionQuestion: If baseline firing rates shift but tuning structure remains, what does that suggest?
steps.stepObjective: Explain common drift causes and choose a first recalibration move.
steps.explanation: Neural features shift across minutes, days, and sessions. Calibration keeps the decoder aligned with the user's current signal before teams assume hardware failure.
steps.oneLineMaster: Decoder drift means yesterday's neural mapping may need today's recalibration.

steps.title: Closed-loop feedback
steps.reflectionQuestion: Which is more dangerous in this case: a false positive or a missed event?
steps.stepObjective: Practice the safety trade-off between detection, action, and feedback.
steps.explanation: Closed-loop systems sense, decide, act, and update. In neurotechnology, feedback must be useful without becoming unsafe, noisy, or too automatic for the user to understand.
steps.oneLineMaster: Closed-loop BCI design links sensing, decoding, action, and feedback.

steps.title: Human-centered BCI UX
steps.checkpointPrompt: Checkpoint B: What makes a closed-loop BCI safe? What could go wrong with false positives? How should the user stay in control?
steps.reflectionQuestion: What should the interface reveal when calibration changes or confidence drops?
steps.stepObjective: Treat feedback, latency, fatigue, calibration transparency, and autonomy as part of the BCI system.
steps.explanation: A BCI user needs clear feedback, low latency, manageable fatigue, understandable calibration, and real control over device behavior. The interface is not decoration; it is part of the neural feedback loop.
steps.oneLineMaster: A BCI is not only a decoder; it is a feedback system between a person and a machine.

steps.title: Brain data safety and ethics
steps.reflectionQuestion: What data would you refuse to collect unless the user clearly opted in?
steps.stepObjective: Apply privacy, consent, minimization, and mental-data safety to BCI design.
steps.explanation: BCI data can reveal sensitive information about attention, intent, and state. Privacy and consent belong in the design, not at the end.
steps.oneLineMaster: Brain data safety means minimizing, protecting, and explaining neural data use.

steps.title: Population vectors & linear readouts
steps.reflectionQuestion: Why does combining evidence from many neurons give a more stable signal than any single neuron?
steps.stepObjective: Understand how a population of neurons can be represented as a vector of features for decoding.
steps.explanation: Real BCIs rarely rely on one neuron. They combine activity across a population into a feature vector. This is the foundation of almost every modern decoder.
steps.oneLineMaster: A population vector turns many noisy neurons into one useful evidence signal.

steps.title: Weighted linear decoder
