steps.reflectionQuestion: If you can see and change the weights, how would you fix a decoder that is biased toward one direction?
steps.stepObjective: Build and tune a real weighted linear readout: score = w1·f1 + w2·f2 + ... and evaluate accuracy.
steps.explanation: A linear decoder is a set of learned weights applied to features. The learner can now inspect, adjust, and see the effect on held-out 'trials'.
steps.oneLineMaster: A linear decoder is just a weighted sum of features followed by a decision rule.

steps.title: Online adaptation & recalibration
steps.checkpointPrompt: Checkpoint C: Given drifting features, what is the smallest change that restores performance without full retraining?
steps.reflectionQuestion: When should you just shift the baseline versus re-learn the entire weight vector?
steps.stepObjective: Distinguish baseline offset correction from full model re-fit and choose the right first action.
steps.explanation: Neural signals drift. Good systems detect the drift and apply the cheapest fix that restores control (baseline, gain, or full recal).
steps.oneLineMaster: Adaptation keeps yesterday's decoder useful today with the smallest possible change.

steps.title: Latency, timing budgets & user adaptation
steps.reflectionQuestion: If every extra 100 ms of latency costs 15 % accuracy in your simulation, where would you spend engineering effort first?
steps.stepObjective: Quantify the cost of latency and understand how users adapt their strategy when feedback is delayed.
steps.explanation: Real BCIs have hard timing budgets. Latency changes both the decoder's job and the user's motor strategy.
steps.oneLineMaster: Latency is not just annoying — it changes the neural signals the decoder must learn.

steps.title: Confidence, error handling & graceful degradation
steps.checkpointPrompt: Checkpoint D: When the decoder reports low confidence, what should the interface do instead of silently acting?
steps.reflectionQuestion: Which is worse for user trust: a wrong action or a clear 'I am uncertain, please confirm' state?
steps.stepObjective: Design simple confidence-aware behavior and graceful fallback when the neural evidence is weak.
steps.explanation: A good BCI never hides uncertainty. It slows down, asks for confirmation, or hands control back to the user.
steps.oneLineMaster: Confidence is not a number — it is a contract with the user about when the system will and will not act.

steps.title: Safety engineering in closed-loop systems
steps.reflectionQuestion: If a false positive triggers stimulation, what single safety rule would you add first?
steps.stepObjective: Apply asymmetric cost thinking and basic interlocks to closed-loop neurotechnology.
steps.explanation: When the output is physical (stimulation, movement, speech), the cost of false positives and false negatives is no longer symmetric.
steps.oneLineMaster: Safety in closed-loop BCIs is about making dangerous mistakes much harder than useful ones.

steps.title: Data architecture, privacy & regulatory reality
steps.reflectionQuestion: If raw neural data must leave the device for training, what minimum protections must be in place?
steps.stepObjective: Reason about on-device vs cloud trade-offs, consent versioning, and high-level regulatory expectations for neural data.
steps.explanation: Neural data is special category data. Architecture, consent, and re-identification risk must be designed in from the start.
steps.oneLineMaster: Privacy and regulatory constraints are first-class engineering requirements, not after-the-fact paperwork.

steps.title: Unit 20 Capstone: full toy BCI integration & reflection
steps.checkpointPrompt: Final Reflection: Assemble everything you have built (viewer → detector → weighted decoder → cursor) and write one honest paragraph on what would still be required to make this system useful for a real person.
steps.reflectionQuestion: What single engineering or human-factor gap between your toy pipeline and a real implanted BCI feels most important to close next?
steps.stepObjective: Produce a documented end-to-end toy BCI pipeline and a clear-eyed reflection on its limitations versus real clinical systems.
steps.explanation: The capstone is not a new algorithm. It is the disciplined integration of everything learned, plus the intellectual honesty to say what is still missing.
steps.oneLineMaster: A real BCI engineer can build the toy, explain why it is still a toy, and know exactly what the next real step would be.

simulations.title: Spike or Noise?
simulations.scenario: You're recording from a Utah array in motor cortex. One channel shows large negative deflections every 200 ms — but they always coincide with the patient's heartbeat on the ECG monitor.
simulations.signalDescription: Periodic ~5 Hz deflections, coincident with QRS complex on ECG, similar shape across multiple electrodes simultaneously.
simulations.decisionQuestion: What do you do with this channel?

choices.label: Treat as cardiac artifact and reject for spike analysis
choices.rationale: Correct — synchrony with ECG and across electrodes points to a non-neural source. Reject or notch out cardiac components.

choices.label: Lower the spike threshold to catch more events
choices.rationale: Weaker — this would let in even more artifact. Threshold is not the underlying problem.

choices.label: Increase stimulation to mask the signal
choices.rationale: Unsafe — stimulation should not be used to mask recording artifacts; you'd inject current without clinical justification.

choices.label: Replace the electrode
choices.rationale: Premature — cardiac artifact is common and software-removable; surgery is not the first step.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Synchrony across channels and with ECG = artifact, not a neuron.

simulations.title: Why Is the Cursor Drifting?
simulations.scenario: A BCI user trained yesterday performs beautifully then. Today, the cursor drifts upward even when they 'rest.' Electrodes are intact and impedance is normal.
simulations.signalDescription: Mean firing rates across the population are ~15% higher than yesterday's baseline; tuning structure is preserved.

choices.label: Recalibrate the decoder with today's neural baseline
choices.rationale: Correct — neural representations drift across days. A short re-cal restores accurate mapping.

choices.label: Schedule revision surgery for new electrodes
choices.rationale: Unsafe — impedances are normal; surgery is unjustified for a likely recalibration issue.

choices.label: Disable the cursor and rely on eye gaze
choices.rationale: Weaker — abandons the BCI prematurely without addressing the cause.

choices.label: Increase decoder gain
choices.rationale: Weaker — amplifies the drift rather than correcting the baseline.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Daily neural drift is normal — recalibrate before assuming hardware failure.

simulations.title: The 90% Decoder That Doesn't Work
simulations.scenario: Your decoder reports 90% offline accuracy, but the user can barely move the cursor. You used a random 80/20 split across all trials, and normalization was computed on the full dataset.
simulations.signalDescription: Offline R² = 0.90 on held-out split; live cursor success rate = 25%.

choices.label: Data leakage and overfitting — random shuffling plus full-data normalization leaked test info into training
choices.rationale: Correct — preprocessing using the full set and random shuffles is a textbook leakage pattern.

choices.label: The patient is uncooperative
choices.rationale: Unsafe — blaming the user without ruling out methodology is wrong both ethically and scientifically.

choices.label: The cursor pixels are too small
choices.rationale: Weaker — visual size doesn't explain a 65-point gap between offline and online performance.

choices.label: Surgery missed the target
choices.rationale: Unsafe and premature — you should fix evaluation methodology before revising surgery.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Great offline numbers + bad online performance = check your splits and preprocessing first.

simulations.title: DBS Programming Trade-off
simulations.scenario: A Parkinson's patient's tremor responds well at 3.2 V on contact 1, but reports tingling in the contralateral hand and some slurred speech. Tremor is fully suppressed only at this amplitude.
simulations.signalDescription: Therapy window: tremor disappears at 2.6 V but returns within 20 seconds of rest. Side effects begin at 3.0 V.

choices.label: Try a different contact or interleaved stimulation to widen the therapy window
choices.rationale: Correct — switching contacts shifts the current field and can decouple therapy from side effects.

choices.label: Increase amplitude further to overpower the side effects
choices.rationale: Unsafe — exceeds the side-effect threshold; charge density may approach safety limits.

choices.label: Turn the device off entirely
choices.rationale: Weaker — abandons known therapy without exploring tunable alternatives.

choices.label: Tell the patient to live with the slurring
choices.rationale: Unsafe and unethical — alternatives haven't been tried yet.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Widen the therapy window before pushing through side effects.

simulations.title: Trigger-Happy Detector
simulations.scenario: An epilepsy responsive stim device triggers ~80 times a day. The patient feels constant subtle paresthesias. EEG review shows most triggers don't precede any seizure activity.
simulations.signalDescription: Detector sensitivity 98%, specificity 60%. Daily triggers: 80. Daily true seizure events: 2.

choices.label: Raise the detection threshold to improve specificity, accepting some sensitivity loss
choices.rationale: Correct — the cost of frequent unnecessary stim outweighs the marginal gain from very high sensitivity.

choices.label: Lower the threshold to catch even more
choices.rationale: Unsafe — makes false positives worse and increases unnecessary stimulation.

choices.label: Increase stimulation amplitude
choices.rationale: Weaker — doesn't address why the device fires inappropriately.

choices.label: Turn detection off entirely
choices.rationale: Unsafe — patient loses seizure protection.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Closed-loop tuning balances sensitivity and specificity — both errors have a real cost.

simulations.title: Prosthetic Hand Without Sensation
simulations.scenario: A user with a myoelectric prosthesis can grip well in the lab. At home they crush eggs and drop heavier objects. They report exhaustion from constantly watching their hand.
simulations.signalDescription: Grip force is binary high/low with no proprioceptive feedback to the user.

choices.label: Add graded sensory feedback (force and contact) via vibrotactile or peripheral nerve stim
choices.rationale: Correct — restoring feedback closes the loop and reduces visual attention load dramatically.

choices.label: Make the grip stronger by default
choices.rationale: Unsafe — stronger uncontrolled grip increases the crushing problem.

choices.label: Train the user to look more carefully
choices.rationale: Weaker — cognitive load is the bottleneck; more vigilance won't scale.

choices.label: Limit the prosthesis to flat objects
choices.rationale: Weaker — limits function without addressing the missing feedback channel.

simulations.oneLineMaster: When users have to watch a prosthesis, sensation is what's missing.

simulations.title: DBS Patient with New Symptoms
simulations.scenario: A long-time DBS patient calls reporting sudden return of tremor. They missed last week's medication refill and recently went through airport security.
simulations.signalDescription: Last clinic impedances normal. Device state: unknown remotely.

choices.label: Bring patient in to reproduce symptoms, verify device on/off state, check impedance, and review meds
choices.rationale: Correct — systematic differential before any parameter change.

choices.label: Immediately schedule revision surgery
choices.rationale: Unsafe — surgery without diagnosis.

choices.label: Tell the patient to double their amplitude at home
choices.rationale: Unsafe — uncontrolled change without diagnosis or supervision.

choices.label: Assume medication non-adherence and dismiss
choices.rationale: Weaker — could miss device-off-event from airport magnet or hardware issue.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Diagnose before you tune — implant troubleshooting is a differential, not a guess.

simulations.title: Reading a Firing Rate Model
simulations.scenario: A rate model of a decision-making circuit has two populations, each with τ = 100 ms, mutual inhibition, and self-excitation. After a step input the rates split: one rises, the other falls.
simulations.signalDescription: Within ~300 ms, one population stabilizes at high rate, the other near zero.
simulations.decisionQuestion: Which mechanism best explains the split?

choices.label: Winner-take-all attractor dynamics from mutual inhibition + self-excitation
choices.rationale: Correct — this is the canonical attractor mechanism for decisions.

choices.label: Hodgkin-Huxley action potentials at the population level
choices.rationale: Weaker — rate models abstract over spikes; HH is the wrong level of description.

choices.label: Synaptic depression alone
choices.rationale: Weaker — depression alone doesn't produce stable attractor states.

choices.label: Random noise without recurrent dynamics
choices.rationale: Weaker — noise alone wouldn't split into stable high/low states.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Mutual inhibition + self-excitation = winner-take-all — a decision attractor.

simulations.title: Lesion in the Wrong Place?
simulations.scenario: A patient has profound naming difficulty after a small stroke. Their MRI shows a tiny lesion in the left thalamus — far from classic language cortex.
simulations.signalDescription: Lesion volume small; behavioral deficit large; canonical language cortex (Broca's, Wernicke's) is structurally intact.

choices.label: Diaschisis — the thalamic lesion disconnects or de-tunes intact language cortex through network effects
choices.rationale: Correct — distant lesions can produce large deficits via network connectivity.

choices.label: The MRI must be misread
choices.rationale: Weaker — assumes imaging error before considering network explanations.

choices.label: Language must be entirely in the thalamus
choices.rationale: Weaker — contradicts decades of lesion and stimulation evidence.

choices.label: The patient is malingering
choices.rationale: Unsafe — clinically inappropriate without thorough workup.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Distant lesions can cause big deficits — think networks, not just hotspots.

simulations.title: Signals Fade After 6 Months
simulations.scenario: A Utah array's signal-to-noise on most channels has dropped 40% over six months. Impedances have crept up. Patient reports no new symptoms.
simulations.signalDescription: Spike amplitude reduced; LFPs still measurable; impedance ~2× baseline on affected channels.

choices.label: Foreign-body response — glial encapsulation insulating the contacts
choices.rationale: Correct — slow rise in impedance with signal degradation is classic chronic encapsulation.

choices.label: Battery problem
choices.rationale: Weaker — recording quality issues with rising impedance aren't usually battery-related.

choices.label: Software bug in the decoder
choices.rationale: Weaker — software doesn't change impedance measurements.

choices.label: Electrode migration into ventricle
choices.rationale: Weaker — would show on imaging and typically presents more abruptly.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Slow impedance creep + amplitude drop = chronic tissue response, not a quick fix.

simulations.title: Choosing a Rehab Device
simulations.scenario: A stroke patient with partial hand function plateaus in outpatient therapy. They have motor cortex sparing but minimal voluntary finger extension. You can recommend one home device.
simulations.signalDescription: EMG shows some volitional activity; minimal finger extension; high motivation.

choices.label: BCI-FES or EMG-triggered FES for hand opening — pairs intent with movement
choices.rationale: Correct — closing the loop between intent and movement drives plasticity-relevant practice.

choices.label: Passive continuous motion device that moves the hand without patient effort
choices.rationale: Weaker — passive practice yields less plasticity than active engagement.

choices.label: No device — wait for spontaneous recovery
choices.rationale: Weaker — plasticity benefits from targeted practice during the recovery window.

choices.label: Major surgical intervention
choices.rationale: Unsafe — premature without trying lower-risk options.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Match the device to remaining intent — pairing volitional drive with movement is what builds recovery.

simulations.title: Selling Attention Scores
simulations.scenario: A wearable EEG headset company offers users free hardware in exchange for the right to sell 'aggregated attention scores' to advertisers and employers.
simulations.signalDescription: Continuous EEG, derived scores updated every minute, consent given as a clickwrap during sign-up.

choices.label: Inferential exposure of mental states to third parties without specific, ongoing consent — a neurorights violation
choices.rationale: Correct — mental privacy and data minimization principles are both breached.

choices.label: The headset is uncomfortable
choices.rationale: Weaker — comfort is a real issue but not the ethical core.

choices.label: Advertisers won't pay much
choices.rationale: Weaker — economic feasibility is not the central ethical problem.

choices.label: EEG can't measure attention at all
choices.rationale: Weaker — overstates; the issue is consent and use, not measurement validity.

simulations.oneLineMaster: Inferring mental states for sale without specific consent is the bright line — mental privacy first.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Variables: Membrane Voltage and Threshold
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A variable is a named value you can reuse. In neuroengineering, variables let you label measurements such as membrane voltage, threshold, or spike count so the logic stays readable.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
# Store two measurements in variables.
membrane_voltage_mv = -55
firing_threshold_mv = -50

print("Voltage:", membrane_voltage_mv)
print("Threshold:", firing_threshold_mv)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Create a variable named resting_voltage_mv with a value of -70 and print it.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Use the pattern name = value, then pass the name to print().
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Variables turn neural measurements into named values your program can reason about.

neuroCodeLessons.title: If-Statements: Did the Neuron Fire?
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: An if-statement lets code choose a path. A simple neuron model can use one to decide whether voltage crossed a firing threshold.
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Change the voltage to -60 and predict the printed result.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: -60 is below -50, so the condition is false.
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: If-statements model decisions such as whether a neural signal crossed threshold.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Lists: Neural Signal Samples
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A list stores many values in order. Neural signals are often sampled over time, so a list can represent a short voltage trace.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
signal_mv = [-70, -68, -61, -48, -54, -66]

print("First sample:", signal_mv[0])
print("Number of samples:", len(signal_mv))
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Add one more sample, -72, to the list and print the new length.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
signal_mv = [-70, -68, -61, -48, -54, -66]
signal_mv.append(-72)
print("Number of samples:", len(signal_mv))
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Lists let code hold a neural time series instead of one isolated measurement.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Loops: Count Spikes in a Trace
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A loop repeats the same logic over many samples. That is exactly what you need when scanning a signal for threshold crossings.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
signal_mv = [-70, -55, -42, -66, -44, -39, -71]
threshold_mv = -50
spike_count = 0

for sample in signal_mv:
    if sample >= threshold_mv:
        spike_count = spike_count + 1

print("Spikes:", spike_count)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Change the threshold to -45. How many spikes are counted now?
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Only samples at or above -45 count.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
signal_mv = [-70, -55, -42, -66, -44, -39, -71]
threshold_mv = -45
spike_count = 0

for sample in signal_mv:
    if sample >= threshold_mv:
        spike_count = spike_count + 1

print("Spikes:", spike_count)
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Loops turn a single signal rule into a scan across an entire recording.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Functions: Firing Rate Calculation
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A function packages reusable logic. Firing rate is a perfect example: count spikes, divide by recording time, reuse the calculation anywhere.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
def firing_rate_hz(spike_count, duration_seconds):
    return spike_count / duration_seconds

rate = firing_rate_hz(24, 3)
print("Firing rate:", rate, "Hz")
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Use the function to calculate the firing rate for 15 spikes in 5 seconds.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
def firing_rate_hz(spike_count, duration_seconds):
    return spike_count / duration_seconds

rate = firing_rate_hz(15, 5)
print("Firing rate:", rate, "Hz")
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Functions make neuroengineering calculations repeatable and easier to trust.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Plotting Concept: EEG-Like Time Series
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: Plots help you inspect signal shape, timing, and artifacts. The MVP does not run Python yet, but this shows the plotting pattern used in notebooks.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
time_seconds = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
eeg_uv = [2, 5, -1, -4, 1]

# In a Python notebook:
# plot(time_seconds, eeg_uv)

print("Plot x-axis:", time_seconds)
print("Plot y-axis:", eeg_uv)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Describe what the x-axis and y-axis represent in this signal plot.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: The x-axis is time. The y-axis is signal amplitude.
neuroCodeLessons.solution: The x-axis is time in seconds, and the y-axis is EEG-like voltage in microvolts.
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Plotting turns arrays of neural samples into shapes your eyes can debug.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Noise and Smoothing: Clean a Neural Signal
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: Signals are noisy. A simple moving average smooths local jumps, making slow trends easier to see while sacrificing some detail.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
signal = [10, 12, 50, 13, 11]

smoothed_middle = (signal[1] + signal[2] + signal[3]) / 3

print("Raw middle sample:", signal[2])
print("Smoothed middle:", smoothed_middle)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Explain why smoothing can help and why it can also hide fast spikes.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Averages reduce extremes by mixing each point with its neighbors.
neuroCodeLessons.solution: Smoothing reduces sudden noise by averaging nearby samples, but it can blur true fast events such as spikes.
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Smoothing trades fast detail for a cleaner view of the signal trend.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Feature Extraction: Building a Real Feature Vector
neuroCodeLessons.neuroengineeringConcept: Population activity as a compact vector (count + energy)
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A real decoder never sees the raw voltage trace. It receives a small feature vector — typically spike count or multi-unit activity per channel (or sorted unit), RMS or band power, and sometimes LFP features. In high-channel arrays this vector can be hundreds of dimensions before dimensionality reduction. These compact numbers are what the linear (or lightly adaptive) decoder actually consumes.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
signal = [2, -1, 8, 3, -2, 9, 1, 4]   # toy 8-sample window (real systems use per-channel or per-unit windows)

spike_count = 0
for v in signal:
    if v > 5:
        spike_count += 1

rms = (sum(v*v for v in signal) / len(signal)) ** 0.5

print("spike_count:", spike_count)
print("rms:", round(rms, 2))
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Add a third feature called 'energy_above_mean' that counts how many samples are above the mean of the window. In real pipelines you would compute this per channel across an array.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: First compute mean, then count how many values are strictly greater than it.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
signal = [2, -1, 8, 3, -2, 9, 1, 4]
mean = sum(signal) / len(signal)
energy_above_mean = sum(1 for v in signal if v > mean)
print("energy_above_mean:", energy_above_mean)
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: A feature vector (often per-channel in high-density arrays) turns raw signal into the small list of numbers a decoder actually sees.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Weighted Linear Decoder: Tuning a Real Readout
neuroCodeLessons.neuroengineeringConcept: Population vector to binary intent via learned weights
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A linear decoder computes score = w_left * feat_left + w_right * feat_right + bias. The weights are the 'votes' each feature gets. The learner can now change the weights and immediately see the effect on accuracy across several 'trials'.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
feat_left = 4.0
feat_right = 9.0

w_left = 1.0
w_right = 1.5
bias = -3.0

score = w_left * feat_left + w_right * feat_right + bias
command = "right" if score > 0 else "left"

print("score:", round(score, 2))
print("command:", command)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Change only the weights (not the features) so the decoder now outputs 'left' on this trial. Then add a second trial with different features and report accuracy on both.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Make w_left much larger than w_right or make bias strongly negative.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
feat_left = 4.0
feat_right = 9.0
w_left = 3.0
w_right = 0.5
bias = -12.0

score = w_left * feat_left + w_right * feat_right + bias
command = "right" if score > 0 else "left"
print("score:", round(score, 2))
print("command:", command)
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: A linear decoder is a weighted vote across features plus a bias. You can see and tune every vote.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Weighted Linear Decoder with Held-Out Evaluation
neuroCodeLessons.neuroengineeringConcept: Seeing overfitting vs generalization on synthetic BCI trials
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: You now have two sets of trials: training (used to choose weights) and held-out test (never seen during tuning). Good weights (often found with ridge/L2 regularization in practice) perform well on both. Overfit weights only look good on the training set. After training you should always inspect the actual weight values — they tell you which features the decoder is truly using.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
# Training trials (you can change weights while looking at these)
train = [(3, 8, "right"), (7, 2, "left"), (4, 9, "right")]

w_left, w_right, bias = 1.0, 1.8, -4.0   # in real code you would add a small L2 penalty on the weights

def predict(fl, fr):
    return "right" if w_left*fl + w_right*fr + bias > 0 else "left"

correct_train = sum(1 for fl, fr, label in train if predict(fl, fr) == label)
print("train accuracy:", correct_train, "/", len(train))
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Add a held-out test set with 4 trials. Tune the weights on the training set only (try a small L2-style penalty in your head), then report accuracy on the unseen test set. Can you get >75 % on test while keeping weights reasonable?
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Do not look at the test labels while choosing w_left / w_right / bias.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
train = [(3, 8, "right"), (7, 2, "left"), (4, 9, "right")]
test  = [(2, 5, "right"), (8, 3, "left"), (1, 10, "right"), (6, 1, "left")]

w_left, w_right, bias = 1.2, 1.6, -5.5   # tuned only on train (in practice add L2 regularization)

def predict(fl, fr):
    return "right" if w_left*fl + w_right*fr + bias > 0 else "left"

correct_test = sum(1 for fl, fr, label in test if predict(fl, fr) == label)
print("test accuracy:", correct_test, "/", len(test))
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: A decoder is only as good as its performance on unseen data. Always inspect the weights after training or adaptation.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Closed-Loop Rule: Detect and Stimulate
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: Closed-loop systems sense, decide, and act. A beginner rule can detect abnormal activity and trigger stimulation only when needed.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
line_length = 18.5
alarm_threshold = 15.0
stimulation_on = False

if line_length > alarm_threshold:
    stimulation_on = True

print("Stimulate:", stimulation_on)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Add a safety rule that stimulation turns on only if battery_percent is above 20.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Use an and condition with line_length and battery_percent.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
line_length = 18.5
alarm_threshold = 15.0
battery_percent = 80
stimulation_on = False

if line_length > alarm_threshold and battery_percent > 20:
    stimulation_on = True

print("Stimulate:", stimulation_on)
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Closed-loop code connects a signal feature to a cautious device action.

neuroCodeLessons.title: Decoder Drift: Recalibrate a Cursor Baseline
neuroCodeLessons.explanation: A cursor decoder can drift when the resting neural feature changes across sessions (baseline offset, gain change, or slow tuning shifts). A simple baseline correction subtracts today's resting value. Real teams often combine this with a slow recursive update (e.g., exponential moving average on the weights or per-channel baseline) that continuously tracks gradual drift without requiring the user to stop and recalibrate.
neuroCodeLessons.codeExample:
# Yesterday's decoder expected rest near 2.0.
old_rest_power = 2.0

# Today's calibration says rest is higher.
today_rest_power = 3.5
observed_power = 6.0

# Correct the observed feature before decoding.
baseline_shift = today_rest_power - old_rest_power
corrected_power = observed_power - baseline_shift

if corrected_power > 4.0:
    command = "move cursor right"
else:
    command = "hold still"

print(command)
neuroCodeLessons.challengePrompt: Change observed_power so the corrected command becomes hold still. In a real system you would also maintain a slow EMA on the weights or baseline across days.
neuroCodeLessons.hint: Make corrected_power less than or equal to 4.0 after subtracting baseline_shift.
neuroCodeLessons.solution:
old_rest_power = 2.0
today_rest_power = 3.5
observed_power = 5.0

baseline_shift = today_rest_power - old_rest_power
corrected_power = observed_power - baseline_shift

if corrected_power > 4.0:
    command = "move cursor right"
else:
    command = "hold still"

print(command)
neuroCodeLessons.oneLineMaster: Recalibration (baseline correction + slow recursive updates) keeps decoder thresholds aligned with today's neural baseline and slow drift.

1.whyItMatters: Every BCI begins by sampling neural activity over time. Code cannot detect spikes, smooth noise, or decode intent until the signal is represented as ordered samples.
1.shortLesson: A neural recording is not one number. It is a sequence: sample 1, sample 2, sample 3, each tied to a time point. Voltage changes mean different things depending on when they happen and how they relate to nearby samples. In toy BCI code, this usually becomes a list or array of values.
1.mentalModel: Think of neural data like a movie made of voltage frames. One frame is rarely enough; the pattern across frames is what the decoder reads.
1.workedExample: If samples are [-70, -68, -45, -42, -70] mV, the middle values only look spike-like because they rise sharply relative to the surrounding samples.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Explain why a neural signal is more like a movie than a photograph.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: A neural signal changes over time, so the order and timing of samples carry information.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What does a sampling rate tell your code?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: It connects sample index to time.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It tells how many measurements are taken per second, which lets code convert sample counts into time windows.

1.commonMistake: Treating one voltage value as meaningful without asking what came before and after it.
1.masteryCriteria: You can describe samples, time windows, and why ordered data is the starting point for BCI code.

2.whyItMatters: Spike detection is often the first transformation from raw voltage to useful neural events. Bad thresholds create fake spikes or miss real ones.
2.shortLesson: A spike detector usually checks whether a waveform crosses a threshold and has a plausible shape. The hard part is not writing `if voltage > threshold`; it is choosing a threshold that separates neural events from noise and artifact.
2.mentalModel: Spike detection is a gate. Set the gate too low and noise walks in; set it too high and real spikes get locked out.
2.workedExample: With threshold -50 mV, samples [-70, -62, -48, -44, -69] include a threshold crossing. If noise also reaches -49 mV, code needs more evidence than threshold alone.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What happens if a spike threshold is too low?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Noise or artifact can be counted as spikes.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why might threshold crossing alone be unsafe?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Large non-neural artifacts can cross the same threshold as real spikes.

2.commonMistake: Lowering the threshold until the output looks busy, even if the events are mostly noise.
2.masteryCriteria: You can explain threshold crossings, false positives, and false negatives in spike detection.

3.whyItMatters: Firing rate turns raw spike events into a stable number a decoder can use for movement intent or state estimation.
3.shortLesson: Firing rate means spikes per unit time. A decoder often cares less about every exact spike time and more about whether a neuron is firing more or less during a movement or intention window.
3.mentalModel: Firing rate is a speedometer for spiking: how many spikes happened during this time window?
3.workedExample: If 12 spikes occur in 2 seconds, the firing rate is 6 spikes/second, or 6 Hz.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Calculate the firing rate for 20 spikes in 4 seconds.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: 5 Hz.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why might a decoder prefer firing rate over raw spike times?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Firing rate is a compact, stable feature that is easier to compare across windows.

3.commonMistake: Comparing spike counts from different window lengths without converting to rate.
3.masteryCriteria: You can calculate spikes per second and explain why decoders use rate features.

4.whyItMatters: BCI code can only decode intent if the input signal is actually related to intent. Noise and artifacts can make a decoder learn the wrong relationship.
4.shortLesson: Noise is unwanted variation in the signal. It can come from movement, electrical interference, heart activity, muscle activity, electrode instability, or unrelated neural activity. Signal-to-noise ratio asks how much useful signal exists compared with unwanted activity.
4.mentalModel: Noise is like static over a radio message. The message may still be there, but the code needs to know what not to trust.
4.workedExample: If a large deflection appears on many electrodes exactly with the ECG heartbeat, it is more likely cardiac artifact than a single neuron.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Name two non-neural sources of noise.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Movement, ECG, muscle activity, electrical interference, or electrode instability.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why can noise break a decoder?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: The decoder may learn artifact patterns instead of neural patterns related to intent.

4.commonMistake: Assuming every large deflection is neural just because it crosses a threshold.
4.masteryCriteria: You can identify artifact sources and explain why bad signal-to-noise damages decoder reliability.

5.whyItMatters: Filtering and smoothing prepare the signal so feature extraction sees the relevant pattern instead of avoidable noise.
5.shortLesson: Filtering keeps some frequency ranges and suppresses others. Smoothing reduces rapid jumps by averaging nearby samples. Both are preprocessing steps, and both can help or hurt depending on the feature you need.
5.mentalModel: Filtering is a lens. It can make the target clearer, but the wrong lens can blur away the thing you need.
5.workedExample: A moving average may make an LFP trend easier to see, but it can smear the exact timing of a narrow spike.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why might smoothing help an LFP feature?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It reduces small rapid fluctuations so slower network-level patterns are easier to see.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: How could smoothing hurt spike detection?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It can flatten or shift fast spike peaks that the detector needs.

5.commonMistake: Smoothing until the feature you need disappears.
5.masteryCriteria: You can explain preprocessing as cleaning signal while preserving the evidence the decoder needs.

6.whyItMatters: Feature extraction is where raw neural data becomes model-ready evidence. A good feature set often beats a fancier model on bad features.
6.shortLesson: A feature vector is a small set of numbers that summarize the neural evidence relevant to the task. Common useful features include spike count in a window, root-mean-square (RMS) amplitude, and band-power proxies. The decoder will later see only these numbers, not the raw trace.
6.mentalModel: The raw voltage trace is a long movie. A feature vector is the short list of answers to the questions the decoder actually cares about right now.
6.workedExample: In a 200 ms window you compute: spike_count = 4, rms = 12.3, beta_power_proxy = 0.7. These three numbers become the input vector [4, 12.3, 0.7] for the decoder.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is RMS often more useful than raw peak amplitude for LFP features?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Think energy across the whole window.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: RMS captures overall signal strength (energy) rather than being dominated by one outlier sample.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What happens if your feature vector ignores timing within the window?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: You lose phase or sequence information.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: You may still decode average intent, but you lose the ability to detect fast sequences or precise onsets.

6.commonMistake: Extracting dozens of correlated features and hoping the model will figure out which ones matter.
6.masteryCriteria: You can compute a small, meaningful feature vector (count + RMS or band-power proxy) from a window of neural data and explain why each number is there.

7.whyItMatters: A linear decoder is the simplest model that actually learns from data. Understanding weighted sums, bias, and evaluation on held-out data is the foundation of almost every production BCI decoder.
7.shortLesson: A linear decoder computes a score = w₁·f₁ + w₂·f₂ + … + b, then applies a decision rule (threshold or argmax). The weights are the learned 'importance' of each feature. Good weights turn noisy population activity into a reliable command.
7.mentalModel: Each feature votes with a strength (its weight). The total vote plus bias decides the output. Changing one weight visibly changes behavior.
7.workedExample: Features = [spike_count_left=5, spike_count_right=12]. With weights w_left=-0.8, w_right=+1.1 and bias=-1.0 the score is 5.4. Positive score → 'right'. If you flip the weights the decoder inverts.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is a weighted sum more powerful than a single if-statement on one feature?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: It can combine evidence and cancel noise.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It lets the decoder listen to the whole population and automatically down-weight unreliable channels.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What does it mean when a decoder performs well on training trials but poorly on new 'held-out' trials?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: It memorized noise instead of the real pattern.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It overfit. The weights captured quirks of the training set that do not generalize.

7.commonMistake: Treating the decoder as a black box instead of inspecting which features have large positive or negative weights.
7.masteryCriteria: You can write a tiny linear decoder (weighted sum + threshold), adjust the weights by hand, and explain why performance changed on both training and held-out examples.

8.whyItMatters: Cursor control shows the full BCI loop: neural signal, decoding, feedback, user adaptation, and recalibration.
8.shortLesson: A cursor-control BCI does not end at classification. The decoded command moves a cursor, the user sees feedback, and the next neural activity changes based on that feedback. Latency and feedback quality shape learning.
8.mentalModel: Cursor BCI is a loop, not a line: brain to decoder to screen back to brain.
8.workedExample: If the decoder outputs LEFT with 80% confidence, the cursor nudges left; if feedback is delayed, the user may overcorrect.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is feedback part of the BCI system?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: The user changes strategy based on what the cursor does, so feedback affects neural input.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What can latency do to cursor control?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It can make control feel unstable and cause overcorrection.

8.commonMistake: Treating the cursor as just an output instead of part of a feedback loop.
8.masteryCriteria: You can trace a cursor-control BCI loop from signal to feedback.

9.whyItMatters: Calibration keeps the decoder aligned with neural signals that naturally change over time.
9.shortLesson: Decoder drift happens when the relationship between features and commands shifts. Causes include electrode movement, tissue response, changing attention, fatigue, or baseline firing shifts. Calibration checks whether today’s signals still match yesterday’s model.
9.mentalModel: Calibration is tuning the instrument before trusting the measurement.
9.workedExample: If both left and right firing features shift upward by 5 Hz, adjusting baseline may fix the decoder before retraining the whole model.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Name two causes of decoder drift.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Electrode movement, tissue response, fatigue, attention changes, or baseline neural shifts.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What would you check first if cursor drift appears?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Check baseline feature shifts and calibration data before assuming hardware failure.

9.commonMistake: Calling drift a hardware failure before checking baseline or calibration.
9.masteryCriteria: You can detect likely drift and choose a first recalibration move.

10.whyItMatters: Closed-loop systems can act automatically, so false positives and false negatives become safety problems, not just accuracy numbers.
10.shortLesson: A closed-loop system senses, decides, acts, and updates. In neurotechnology, the action might be stimulation, feedback, or device adjustment. Designers must balance sensitivity, specificity, timing, and user control.
10.mentalModel: Closed loop is a thermostat with higher stakes: sense, decide, act, then sense again.
10.workedExample: If abnormal activity detection triggers stimulation too easily, false positives may stimulate when the user did not need it.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What are the four parts of a closed-loop system?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Sense, decide, act, and update/monitor feedback.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why can a false positive be unsafe?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: The system may act when it should not, such as unnecessary stimulation or feedback.

10.commonMistake: Optimizing detection while ignoring what happens after the trigger.
10.masteryCriteria: You can reason about false positives, false negatives, and safety in a closed-loop rule.

11.whyItMatters: A BCI succeeds only if the person can understand, trust, and control the interaction over time.
11.shortLesson: Human-centered BCI UX includes feedback, latency, fatigue, calibration transparency, confidence display, and autonomy. The interface helps the user learn the system and helps the system remain accountable to the user.
11.mentalModel: The UI is part of the neural circuit: it changes what the user tries next.
11.workedExample: If decoder confidence drops, the interface might slow cursor movement and clearly ask for recalibration instead of silently becoming erratic.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is UX not decoration in a BCI?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Feedback and interface choices change user behavior, safety, fatigue, and autonomy.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What should the user know during recalibration?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Why recalibration is happening, what data is used, and how they can stay in control.

11.commonMistake: Thinking UX is separate from decoding instead of part of the feedback system.
11.masteryCriteria: You can explain how latency, fatigue, feedback, and autonomy affect BCI performance.

12.whyItMatters: Brain data can be sensitive because it may reveal attention, intent, state, or patterns the user did not mean to share.
12.shortLesson: Brain data safety means collecting the minimum useful data, explaining what is collected, protecting it, and giving users control. Ethics is not a final slide; it shapes the system design from the beginning.
12.mentalModel: Treat brain data like a private journal with timestamps and sensor metadata.
12.workedExample: A wellness BCI should not sell attention-state scores to advertisers unless the user explicitly understands and opts in.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What does data minimization mean?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Collect only the data needed for the stated purpose.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is brain data privacy different from ordinary app telemetry?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It may reveal sensitive information about attention, intent, mood, or health.

12.commonMistake: Collecting extra neural data just because storage is cheap.
12.masteryCriteria: You can explain consent, minimization, privacy, and user control in BCI data design.

13.whyItMatters: Modern high-channel implants record from hundreds to >1000 neurons simultaneously. The population vector (or its modern descendant: a compact feature vector across channels or sorted units) remains the fundamental representation almost every decoder consumes.
13.shortLesson: A population vector is the list of firing rates or feature values across the recorded population at one time step. In real systems the 'vector' is often per-channel or per-unit features plus LFP band powers; the decoder rarely sees raw spikes or voltage traces.
13.mentalModel: Each neuron is one noisy coordinate. The current evidence for intent is the entire short list of numbers. Classic cosine tuning and modern high-density arrays both reduce to this same idea: the pattern across the population carries the signal.
13.workedExample: On a 64-channel array you compute spike count + RMS in a 100 ms window per channel (or per sorted unit). The resulting ~128-element feature vector is what the linear decoder receives. Real pipelines often add a few LFP band-power features and then reduce dimensionality.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why does combining evidence across 100+ neurons produce a stabler signal than any single neuron?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Individual neurons are noisy and can drop out; the population average or weighted combination cancels much of that variance.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: In a modern high-channel implant, why might the decoder see per-channel features rather than a classic 'population vector' of preferred directions?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: With hundreds of channels the raw population is high-dimensional and non-stationary; engineers extract compact, somewhat stable features per channel or unit first.

13.commonMistake: Assuming the decoder ever receives raw voltage or every spike time. It almost always receives a small engineered feature vector.
13.masteryCriteria: You can explain why a compact feature vector derived from a large neural population is the standard input to almost every practical BCI decoder, and why high-channel arrays still reduce to this same representation.

14.whyItMatters: A linear decoder (often fit with ridge regression) is still one of the most widely deployed decoding approaches in clinical and research BCIs because it is fast, interpretable, and robust when training data is limited. Seeing and changing the weights turns the decoder from magic into an engineering object you can debug and constrain.
14.shortLesson: score = w₁·f₁ + w₂·f₂ + … + b (often with L2 regularization / ridge). The weights are chosen (or adapted) so the score favors the correct intention. Held-out evaluation and direct weight inspection tell you whether the model generalized and which channels or features actually drive the output.
14.mentalModel: Each feature casts a weighted vote. You can look at every vote, raise or lower any voter's influence, and immediately see the effect on both training and held-out performance. Regularization (ridge) keeps individual weights from becoming absurdly large on noisy neural data.
14.workedExample: Features = [left_rate=5, right_rate=11]. With w_left=-0.9, w_right=1.4, bias=-2.0 the score is positive → 'right'. After training you inspect the weights: the large positive w_right tells you the right-side feature is the dominant driver. If a channel's weight is near zero, you can often drop or mask it with little loss.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What does a large positive weight on a feature tell you about that feature's relationship to the decoded variable?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: It votes strongly for one class.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: When that feature is high, the decoder is strongly pulled toward the positive class.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: If your linear decoder gets 95 % on the trials you used to choose the weights but only 60 % on new trials, what almost certainly happened?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: It overfit. The weights captured quirks of the particular training set that do not appear in fresh data. Ridge (L2) regularization is the usual practical fix.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is being able to inspect the actual weight values after training a safety and debugging advantage in a real BCI?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: You can see which channels or features are driving decisions, detect when a single bad channel has taken over, and make principled decisions about masking or re-weighting.

14.commonMistake: Never looking at the actual weight values after training or after an online update.
14.masteryCriteria: You can write and tune a weighted linear decoder (with basic regularization intuition), inspect the weights, and explain performance on both the data used for tuning and on held-out trials.

15.whyItMatters: In any chronically implanted BCI, the relationship between neural activity and the features you extract will change over days and weeks. Understanding why and how to respond is one of the most important real-world engineering skills.
15.shortLesson: Drift has physical and statistical causes: electrode micromotion, gliosis/encapsulation (impedance rise), reference changes, and behavioral or learning-induced shifts. Different signatures (global offset, per-channel gain, tuning rotation / covariate shift) call for different responses. Good systems first detect the change (performance drop, confidence distribution shift, impedance rise) then apply the cheapest fix that restores control: baseline/gain correction, per-channel re-weighting, or a lightweight recursive update (e.g., exponential moving average on the weights or features). Full supervised re-fits or more sophisticated continual learning are used when cheap methods fail.
15.mentalModel: The mapping from neural activity to intent is not stationary. You must detect when it has changed and respond with the smallest intervention that keeps the user in control. A simple recursive update (exponential moving average on the weight vector or per-channel baseline) can track slow drift without forcing the user into frequent explicit recalibration sessions.
15.workedExample: After 6 weeks, channel 7 shows rising impedance and flattened tuning. A per-channel gain correction restores most performance. You also maintain a slow exponential moving average on the weight vector (learning rate ~0.01) that continuously tracks gradual changes across the population. When performance remains poor after the cheap fixes, you trigger a short supervised recalibration.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Name two physical causes of chronic drift that are largely outside the decoder's control.
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Think about what happens around the electrode over weeks.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Electrode micromotion and the brain's foreign-body response (gliosis) that increases impedance and can dampen or distort the recorded signal.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: How would you notice that drift has begun before the cursor or device output feels obviously wrong?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Monitor task performance trends, the distribution of decoder confidence scores, per-channel impedance or noise floor, and the rate of explicit user corrections or overrides.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: When would you choose a lightweight baseline/gain correction or recursive update versus triggering a full supervised re-fit?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Cost to the user and risk.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Use cheap corrections or slow recursive updates when the change looks like offset, uniform gain, or slow rotation. Trigger a full re-fit (or switch to more aggressive continual adaptation) when tuning curves have changed substantially or performance stays poor after minimal fixes.

15.commonMistake: Treating every performance drop as 'the electrodes failed' and assuming only surgery or total retraining can help. Most of the time a principled, minimal correction is sufficient.
15.masteryCriteria: You can name the main physical and statistical drivers of chronic non-stationarity, distinguish common drift signatures, describe one lightweight adaptive technique (e.g., exponential moving average or per-channel correction), and choose an appropriate minimal response given observed changes.

16.whyItMatters: Every extra millisecond of latency changes both the user's strategy and the statistics the decoder must learn. Timing is a first-class design variable.
16.shortLesson: Real wireless high-channel BCIs target end-to-end latencies often below 80–120 ms from neural event to output (cursor movement or stimulation). Every filtering stage, wireless hop, and decoding step consumes the budget. Added latency forces the user to predict further ahead, changes the statistics the decoder must learn, and often makes slower, larger modulations more effective than fast ones.
16.mentalModel: The cursor is always a little bit in the past. The user learns to aim where the cursor will be, not where it is. The decoder must therefore learn the user's predictive strategy, not their instantaneous intent.
16.workedExample: At 80 ms latency the user can still use relatively fast changes in firing rate. At 250 ms the same user starts producing slower, more deliberate modulations because fast corrections arrive too late.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why does added latency often make users produce slower, larger neural modulations?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: They have to plan further ahead.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Fast corrections become useless; the user learns to make bigger, earlier changes that the delayed feedback can still influence.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: If you can only spend engineering effort on one thing — better features or 40 ms lower latency — which one usually gives more bit-rate in a real cursor task?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Lower latency almost always wins at the margin because it changes the user's strategy and the statistics of the problem itself.

16.commonMistake: Treating latency as a pure engineering annoyance instead of a variable that reshapes the neural signals the decoder must learn.
16.masteryCriteria: You can explain how latency changes user behavior and why it is often more valuable to reduce delay than to add another clever feature.

17.whyItMatters: A decoder that occasionally acts when it should not can be worse than one that sometimes refuses to act. Confidence and graceful degradation are safety and trust features, not UI polish.
17.shortLesson: When the evidence is weak or contradictory, a good system slows down, widens its acceptance window, or explicitly asks the user for confirmation instead of guessing and being wrong.
17.mentalModel: The system has a 'I am not sure' state. In that state it asks for help or does nothing rather than risking a costly mistake.
17.workedExample: If the linear score is only +0.3 (just above threshold) the cursor moves very slowly or the interface pops up 'Confirm right?'. The user can cancel or strengthen the command.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between 'the decoder is uncertain' and 'the user changed their mind'?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: One is epistemic, one is intentional.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Uncertainty means the neural evidence is weak or noisy. Mind change means the user now wants the opposite action. Good interfaces try to distinguish the two.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why can a system that 'always does something' actually reduce the user's sense of control?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Wrong actions feel like loss of agency.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Every incorrect action teaches the user that the device sometimes acts against their will. Occasional explicit 'I don't know' states preserve trust.

17.commonMistake: Optimizing only for accuracy or speed while ignoring the user's subjective experience of being in control.
17.masteryCriteria: You can design simple confidence-aware behaviors and explain why they improve both objective performance and user trust.

18.whyItMatters: When the output of the BCI can cause physical effects (stimulation, movement, speech), the cost of errors is no longer symmetric. Safety engineering becomes a first-class requirement.
18.shortLesson: False positives and false negatives now have real physiological or functional costs. Systems must be designed so that dangerous mistakes are orders of magnitude harder than useful actions. Key controls include charge-density and charge-balance limits per stimulation phase, rate limiting on commands, explicit user interlocks that cannot be overridden by the algorithm, and fail-safe states. Chronic drift can silently move a previously safe operating point into the danger region if detection and compensation are missing.
18.mentalModel: The system has two very different error budgets: one for 'missed useful action' and one for 'harmful action'. The second budget is tiny — and drift can silently move a previously safe system across that line.
18.workedExample: A seizure-detection stimulator was tuned with a threshold that produced <1 false positive per week on month-1 data. By month-3, electrode impedance rise and a small baseline shift on one channel cause the same threshold to trigger 40 unnecessary stimulations per day. The team must either raise the threshold (risking missed events) or add drift compensation + an explicit confirmation step before stimulation.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is a 99 % accurate detector still dangerous if the 1 % errors are all false positives that trigger stimulation?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: One percent of 10 000 daily detections is 100 unnecessary stimulations — potentially unacceptable.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What is the simplest 'interlock' that prevents a closed-loop system from acting while the user is explicitly overriding?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: A button, voice command, or eye-tracking 'stop' signal that the user can assert faster than the automatic loop can act.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: How can undetected drift turn a previously safe closed-loop system into an unsafe one?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Features that used to indicate 'no stimulation needed' can drift into the stimulation region. Without drift detection or compensation, the system will now over-stimulate on what used to be normal activity.

18.commonMistake: Optimizing the detector in isolation and only later discovering that its operating point is unsafe in the real clinical context.
18.masteryCriteria: You can reason about asymmetric error costs and propose at least one concrete safety mechanism (threshold, confirmation, interlock, or override) for a closed-loop neurotechnology application, including how drift could invalidate yesterday's safety assumptions.

19.whyItMatters: Neural data is uniquely sensitive because it supports inference about unstated mental states. Architecture, consent, and re-identification risk must be designed in from day one.
19.shortLesson: Raw neural recordings or high-dimensional feature vectors can be re-identified or used to infer attention, emotion, or intent. On-device processing, data minimization, and explicit consent versioning are not optional. Adding unsupervised drift compensation can constitute a software change that requires risk re-analysis and documentation under design controls (IEC 62304, 21 CFR 820). Wireless implants also face cybersecurity expectations. Chronic drift is therefore both an engineering and a regulatory fact of life.
19.mentalModel: Every piece of neural data you store or transmit is a potential future liability or a future research asset. You must decide which it will be before you collect it — and drift means the system you validated yesterday may no longer behave the same way tomorrow.
19.workedExample: A wellness BCI that uploads raw LFP to the cloud for 'better decoding' also uploads enough information to potentially infer the user's mood, attention, or even some disease markers. That upload now requires a different consent flow and stronger technical protections. If the team later adds automatic drift compensation that changes the effective decoder behavior, they may also trigger additional change-control documentation under software-as-medical-device rules.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why is 'we'll just anonymize the neural data later' usually not a credible plan?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: High-dimensional data is hard to anonymize.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Neural signals are high-dimensional and individual. Simple de-identification is often reversible with auxiliary information.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: What is the practical difference between training a decoder on-device versus sending data to the cloud?
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Raw data exposure and attack surface.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: On-device keeps the raw signal (and therefore the most sensitive inferences) inside the user's physical device. Cloud training requires either sending raw data or using federated / privacy-preserving techniques.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why can unhandled chronic drift create a regulatory or change-control burden even if the underlying hardware has not changed?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: If drift compensation or automatic re-weighting meaningfully alters the decoder's input-output relationship, the system may be considered to have undergone a software change that requires documentation, risk re-analysis, or even re-validation depending on the regulatory jurisdiction.

19.commonMistake: Treating privacy and regulatory requirements as paperwork that happens after the product is built.
19.masteryCriteria: You can articulate at least two concrete technical or process decisions (on-device vs cloud, data minimization, consent versioning, etc.) that materially change the privacy or regulatory risk of a neural data product, including how drift can create ongoing change-control obligations.

20.whyItMatters: The final skill is not building one more algorithm. It is integrating everything you have learned into a coherent, documented toy system and then honestly describing exactly why that toy is still far from a real clinical device — especially the realities of chronic non-stationarity.
20.shortLesson: The Unit 20 capstone is the disciplined assembly of a full toy pipeline (raw signal → feature vector → weighted linear decoder → latency-aware feedback) plus a concise, technically honest reflection. The reflection must name at least two distinct mechanisms of chronic non-stationarity (e.g., impedance rise + gliosis, tuning rotation), describe one detection method and one practical response (baseline correction, recursive update, or selective re-fit), one safety or regulatory implication of that response, and the single most important concrete next engineering step a real team would staff tomorrow.
20.mentalModel: You have built a working toy BCI. Now you must also be able to explain, in one clear page, why no patient would receive this exact system tomorrow — with specific mechanisms of drift, not slogans — and what the real next engineering or clinical step would be.
20.workedExample: Your integrated notebook accepts a short neural recording, extracts your 3-element feature vector, runs your weighted linear decoder, drives a cursor with latency, and logs performance. Your reflection states: 'Two major gaps are (1) gradual impedance rise + gliosis causing differential channel drift that our global baseline correction cannot fully compensate, and (2) lack of any unsupervised adaptation when the user is not explicitly recalibrating. A realistic next step would be to add per-channel gain tracking + a simple recursive least-squares style update triggered by rising low-confidence rate, plus explicit safety bounds on stimulation or cursor velocity during detected drift.'

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Name two distinct physical or statistical mechanisms of chronic non-stationarity that a toy pipeline using only global baseline correction will eventually fail on, plus one lightweight detection method.
activeRecallPrompts.hint: Electrode biology + neural pattern change + monitoring.
activeRecallPrompts.answer: 1) Impedance rise + gliosis causing per-channel attenuation. 2) Tuning rotation / covariate shift. Detection: rising rate of low-confidence outputs or per-channel impedance/noise trends.

activeRecallPrompts.prompt: Why does writing an honest limitations section that names specific drift mechanisms and a concrete next step actually increase (rather than decrease) the credibility of a BCI engineer?
activeRecallPrompts.answer: Anyone can make a demo that works for five minutes. The person who can clearly articulate what would break in the real world — and exactly what the next staffed engineering step would be — is the one teams trust.

20.commonMistake: Declaring the toy 'done' without naming the concrete engineering or clinical work that would be required to make it useful for a real person.
20.masteryCriteria: You have produced a documented, runnable end-to-end toy BCI pipeline and a concise, technically honest reflection that names at least two specific mechanisms of chronic non-stationarity, one detection method, one practical response (e.g., recursive update or per-channel correction), one safety or regulatory implication, and the single most important concrete next engineering step.
